One-time backlog extraction (≥25-min episodes). Standalone — not wired into the dashboard.
Quotes shown “verbatim” are matched in the source transcript; “≈ … paraphrase” means close-but-not-exact (auto-caption drift). Timestamps were not available (transcripts stored as plain text) — every link opens the episode at the start.

Adam Taggart | Thoughtful Money 4 episode(s) · 143 insights

Is The Rally Over? Has The Pullback Now Started? | Lance Roberts102 min37 insights2026-06-06
▶ Watch on YouTube
After 9-10 straight up weeks, Lance Roberts says a correction has technically begun (sell signal triggered, 20-day MA broken) but argues it will likely be a shallow 5-7% rotational pullback — capital sloshing from overbought semis/mega-cap tech into lagging value/quality/dividend names rather than leaving the market — unless an oil-supply spike, liquidity tightening, or impaired hyperscaler capex hits forward earnings. Macro backdrop is resilient (capex + deficits + bonus-depreciation juicing growth, earnings revisions surging like a post-recession recovery), but late-cycle warning signs are mounting: record equity issuance/IPO supply, a 15-year/500%+ bull, and AI now cited as the #1 layoff reason.
Correction has technically started — shallow & rotational · 8Resilient macro & surging earnings revisions · 6Cracks in the consumer & sustainability of growth · 4Late-cycle signals: IPO/equity supply & long bull · 4Oil supply, liquidity & rug-pull risks · 5Bitcoin, credit spreads & private credit · 3Labor market split & AI displacement · 3Portfolio trades & risk management · 4
Correction has technically started — shallow & rotational 8
Lance Roberts says the S&P 500 correction has technically begun: a momentum sell signal triggered, the market is working off a severe overbought condition, and price is taking out the 20-day moving average.
thesis🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Confirms near-term downside bias; traders should expect further weakness and manage risk rather than chase.
“By all intents and measures, technically speaking, yes, the correction has started.”
Roberts expects the pullback to be a rotational 'water sloshing in the bathtub' move — capital rotating out of overbought semiconductors/mega-cap tech into lagging value/quality, producing only a 3-7% index decline rather than a deep 10-15% buy-the-dip correction.
prediction🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumS&P 500semiconductorsvalue stocksquality stocks
So what: Implies no big 'buy the dip' bargain in the index; the pain concentrates in tech while value cushions the downside.
“the market may not actually correct in the way that a lot of people are expecting” ▶ 3:45
Because technology and communications (Meta, Google, Nvidia and the semis) together make up ~50% of the S&P 500, and semiconductors alone are ~18%, any correction in those sectors mechanically drags the whole index lower even if money rotates into other sectors.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500Technology sectorCommunications sectorNvidiaMetaGooglesemiconductors
So what: Index-level declines are unavoidable given concentration; cap-weight exposure carries hidden single-sector risk.
≈ “those two sectors so communications is meta and Google and then you know your technology sectors Nvidia all the semiconductors everything else they make up 50% of the S&P 500 index” paraphrase
The rotation is already visible: on the day of taping Micron, AMD and Intel were each down ~7% while value and quality stocks rose, and the 3x semiconductor ETF is up roughly 1,000% from where this rally started — flagging how stretched the semi trade had become.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highMicronAMDIntelsemiconductors3x semiconductor ETF
So what: The unwind of the most-crowded leveraged semi trade can be violent; size and stops matter most there.
“Micron down 7%, AMD down, uh, you know, 7%, Intel's down 7% this morning”
Roberts expects roughly 7,300 on the S&P as minor support, with the 50-day MA (~7,150, a 38.2% retracement) and the 100-day MA (a 50% retracement) as the most realistic downside objectives — a 50% retracement of the rally implying about a 5-7% correction.
prediction🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumS&P 500
So what: Gives concrete levels to watch for re-entry / risk sizing on the pullback.
“the 50-day moving average, which is currently around 7150ish, is going to be your most logical next support”
Roberts warns that after such a long complacent rally, even a normal 3-7% correction will feel like a 10-15% bear-market drop psychologically, and investors should avoid overreacting and selling out entirely.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Behavioral edge: pre-committing to not panic-sell a shallow dip protects against capitulating at the lows.
“It's going to feel like a 10 15% correction psychologically. So, don't overreact to the correction.”
Roberts frames the immediate-term setup as ~2% upside versus ~7% downside (a 3:1 to 4:1 risk/reward), which is why his firm trimmed high-beta tech mid-day and is adding to value — downside risk now far exceeds upside.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Asymmetric payoff argues for de-risking high-beta exposure now rather than reaching for the last few percent.
“the upside was maybe, you know, 2% higher versus a 7% downside”
The two-month rally off the April low is one of the largest two-month advances on record, which Roberts cites as an additional reason the move is exhausted and due to mean-revert.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Historically extreme momentum tends to be followed by give-back; supports taking profits.
“The size of that two-month rally is one of the largest on record.” ▶ 1:18:30
Resilient macro & surging earnings revisions 6
S&P 500 forward EPS growth estimates are being revised up toward ~30% (from below 20% a couple months ago) — a pattern of sharp upward earnings revisions that historically only occurs coming OUT of a recession (2008, dot-com, 2015-16, post-pandemic), yet is happening with no official recession.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500earnings
So what: Either earnings momentum is real and durable, or estimates are over-extended and vulnerable to a sharp reset.
“This is something you only see coming out of recessions.” ▶ 10:20
Roberts and Taggart argue the U.S. effectively went through a 'rolling recession' that was papered over with trillions of stimulus — Roberts' ~100-input economic composite hit recession-level readings around 2023 — which makes today's V-shaped earnings recovery a normal cyclical rebound rather than an anomaly.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highU.S. economyearnings
So what: Reframes the current upswing as mid-cycle recovery, supporting near-term resilience but implying an eventual reversal.
“maybe technically we never had a recession but economically we went through an a recession”
Taggart identifies three simultaneous stimulants 'juicing' the economy: hyperscaler capex (~$800-900B in 2026, >$1T in 2027), aggressive government deficit spending, and the One Big Beautiful Bill's 100% year-one bonus depreciation incentivizing corporate plant/equipment investment.
causal🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: highhyperscalersdata centersU.S. governmentfiscal policybonus depreciation
So what: These are the load-bearing pillars of growth; watch capex guidance and fiscal flows as the key macro tells.
“there there is like multiple shots of adrenaline going into this economy”
An economically-weighted ISM index (70% services / 30% manufacturing) has turned up from borderline-contraction, with ISM manufacturing back above 50 after nearly three years in contraction — confirming broad economic re-acceleration that supports the earnings recovery.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highISM manufacturingISM services
So what: Hard confirmation the recovery is broad, not just AI capex; bears betting on imminent recession face a strong tape.
“the ISM manufacturing index has been in contraction for almost three straight years. It's back into expansion territory”
Multiple independent labor sources (Revelio Labs, Bank of America payrolls estimate, ADP) all show a sharp resurgence in hiring, corroborating the BLS non-farm payrolls uptick — Taggart's point that you can dislike the government data but several private datasets tell the same story.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: mediumBLSRevelio LabsBank of AmericaADPjobs
So what: Don't trade on data-denial; corroborated labor strength reduces near-term recession odds.
“just because you hate one indicator because it's the government... there's a lot of other indicators out there that are also telling you exactly the same thing”
Roberts' core market philosophy: the market only prices the published data, not how you feel about its accuracy — trading on shadow-stats inflation or 'fake' CPI beliefs while the market uses official CPI impairs wealth by keeping you out of rising markets.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highCPIShadowStatsBLS
So what: Discipline edge: position to the data the market actually responds to, not to a 'truer' private metric it ignores.
“Nobody cares what you think when it comes to the financial markets... The market only cares about the data that's published.” ▶ 15:09
Cracks in the consumer & sustainability of growth 4
Q1 GDP (~3%, likely revised to 2-2.5%) was driven by the ~15% business-investment component (data-center capex) while personal consumption (PCE, ~70% of GDP) actually contracted — the consumer is slowing with rising delinquencies and fading debt-fueled spending.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highGDPPCEconsumerdata centers
So what: The growth is narrowly capex-led; consumer weakness is the soft underbelly that could undercut the durability of the boom.
“personal consumption expenditures PCE which is 70% of GDP contracted. So the consumer is slowing down.”
Unit labor costs (largely wages) are declining sharply, which Roberts says will feed through to weaker consumption and drag CPI lower later in 2026 and into 2027 once the oil spike passes.
causal🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumunit labor costswagesCPIconsumer
So what: Disinflation thesis: falling wage costs point to lower future CPI prints and softer demand — bond-supportive, growth-cautionary.
≈ “unit labor costs are declining rather sharply. That's going to drag inflation lower later this year.” paraphrase
The entire bullish narrative is hostage to hyperscaler capex: if credit markets pull back and 2027 capex falls from ~$1.2T to, say, $700B, forward earnings expectations break and the whole market gets hit regardless of the rotation dynamic.
thesis🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: highhyperscalersdata centerscredit marketsearnings
So what: Capex financing conditions are the single biggest tail risk; monitor AI-infrastructure credit and capex guidance closely.
“if those if those get materially impaired, that's going to be a really big deal because so much of those earnings expectations are based on that money getting spent”
Roberts notes strong economic growth and strong market returns are NOT the same: you can have a decent-growth year with an underperforming market depending on valuations — a caution that bullish macro green shoots don't guarantee continued index gains.
framework🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: mediumS&P 500GDPvaluations
So what: Decouples macro optimism from equity allocation; valuation, not GDP, drives forward returns.
“you can still have a year of pretty decent economic growth, but you could have an underperforming market”
Late-cycle signals: IPO/equity supply & long bull 4
A historic surge in equity issuance — a record IPO pipeline (SpaceX, AI/LLM names) plus secondaries like Google's reported ~$85B raise — is, in Roberts' upcoming research, a classic late-cycle supply signal that historically precedes recessionary or crash outcomes (dot-com peak, 2020 SPAC wave).
contrarian🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highIPOsSpaceXGoogleequity issuance
So what: Massive new supply at premium prices needs demand to absorb it; when it can't, prices fall — a top-signal to respect.
“when you have big historic spikes in equity issuance, it tends to correspond with recessionary outcomes in in the markets”
Roberts says the wave of IPOs/issuance is insiders and private equity cashing out at premium valuations into a hot market, and he would NOT buy SpaceX on IPO day despite liking the company long-term — wait rather than pay the IPO-day premium.
thesis🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highSpaceXprivate equityIPOs
So what: Avoid IPO-day premiums on hot offerings; insider selling into strength is a distribution signal.
“if I'm an insider and you got a market doing this, yeah, I'm looking to get out”
Roberts notes 2008's equity-issuance surge should be excluded from the bearish historical analog because that was desperation capital-raising to avoid bankruptcy (e.g., Goldman Sachs selling preferreds to Berkshire), not opportunistic top-ticking like today.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highGoldman SachsBerkshire Hathawayequity issuance
So what: Refines the issuance signal: today's supply is opportunistic distribution, which is the more reliable late-cycle tell.
“they were doing equity issuance to stay alive” ▶ 51:43
Roberts characterizes this as a 15-year, ~500-600% bull market — by magnitude of advancement the biggest ever — and stresses it will end, but momentum is hard to kill and it could run another 2-4 years before reverting.
thesis🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumS&P 500bull market
So what: Stay invested but vigilant; don't short a momentum bull, but recognize late-cycle positioning is warranted.
“by far, it's the biggest bull market we've ever had” ▶ 53:01
Oil supply, liquidity & rug-pull risks 5
Roberts flags oil as the most likely 'rug pull': a supply-driven spike (oil has been flat between ~$90-100 for a month) that erodes consumer confidence, rolls into earnings expectations, and triggers a broad market sell-off — the kind of event that makes capital actually leave the 'bathtub.'
causal🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumoilcrudeconsumer confidenceearnings
So what: An oil-supply shock is the cleanest path from a shallow rotation to a real index correction; watch crude as the trigger.
“there's a lot of complacency in the market... so it won't take a lot to do a rug pull” ▶ 39:47
Taggart warns oil inventories have 'passed the red line' (citing imminent interview with oil expert Jeff Currie and a corroborating WSJ article), so prices could move substantially higher despite the oil market not yet pricing the Iran/Strait-of-Hormuz risk.
prediction🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: mediumoilJeff CurrieIranStrait of HormuzWall Street Journal
So what: Inventory drawdown is a setup for an oil squeeze; energy longs benefit, broad equities at risk if it spikes.
“there's definitely reasons to be concerned here that oil prices could actually start going substantially higher because we've kind of passed the red line in in inventories”
Roberts' RIA portfolio is long energy (Exxon Mobil, MLPs) and would benefit from higher oil; he's 'waiting for the oil market to tell me' to care, treating price action as the signal rather than front-running the inventory narrative.
anecdote🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumExxon MobilMLPsenergyoil
So what: Energy exposure is a hedge against the oil-spike scenario that would hurt the broad market.
“we're long energy stocks. We own Exxon Mobile. We own MLPS... I would love higher oil prices because that would play out great for the portfolio”
Liquidity / financial conditions are about as loose as they've been in a long time, sustained by bank lending surging alongside economic growth and the Fed holding lots of short-term T-bills that fund bank reserves and encourage lending — Roberts says nothing is impairing liquidity yet.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highliquidityFederal ReserveT-billsbank lending
So what: Loose liquidity is the bull's fuel; watch bank-lending slowdown, rate hikes or pricier oil as the things that would choke it.
“liquidity is the blood of the market” ▶ 46:55
Roberts says it would take the Fed hiking, rates rising and staying up, or much higher oil to tighten liquidity — and he does NOT expect the Fed to hike rates, keeping the liquidity backdrop supportive for now.
prediction🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumFederal Reserveinterest ratesliquidity
So what: Absent a Fed hike or oil spike, the liquidity tide stays in — limiting downside to a shallow correction.
“you need the Fed to hike rates... which I don't expect they will” ▶ 45:37
Bitcoin, credit spreads & private credit 3
Bitcoin has fallen sharply with retail enthusiasm gone quiet; the four explanations floated are a normal halving-style cyclical drawdown, Michael Saylor's Strategy (ex-MicroStrategy) selling a small amount of BTC, AI/semiconductors siphoning speculative capital, and Bitcoin signaling waning liquidity as a 'canary in the coal mine.'
framework🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: exploratoryBitcoinMichael SaylorStrategyMicroStrategyAIsemiconductors
So what: If Bitcoin is a liquidity tell, its weakness could foreshadow tightening even while official liquidity reads loose.
“Bitcoin's a basically a measure of liquidity and liquidity must be waning right now”
Credit spreads remain 'dead' (complacent), having only briefly widened on the oil-driven rate spike before tightening again as oil and rates fell — Roberts reads this as no stress signal from credit markets.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highcredit spreadsinterest ratesoil
So what: Tight credit spreads remove a key recession warning; a widening would be the early sign of real trouble.
≈ “Yep. Dead.” paraphrase
Roberts argues the ~$2T private-credit market is too small to be 'the next subprime'; recent fund gating is a liquidity run, not credit impairment — e.g., the Blackstone BDC (BXSL) has ~98% collateralized loans and trades at a discount to NAV, which he frames as opportunity in the 'rubble.'
contrarian🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumprivate creditBlackstoneBDCsBXSL
So what: Discounted, high-quality BDCs may be a contrarian buy; gating ≠ insolvency, but buyers must vet credit quality carefully.
“gating does not necessarily mean that there's something wrong with the underlying credits”
Labor market split & AI displacement 3
The labor data tells two stories: hiring is resurging but quits remain very low (signaling worker fear), the bulk of new jobs are low-wage leisure/hospitality/healthcare, and the college-graduate unemployment rate is an elevated ~5.6% — a 'low hire, no fire' market skewed to low-pay roles.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: highjobsquits rateleisurehospitalityhealthcarecollege graduates
So what: The headline strength masks a soft, low-quality job mix — consumer spending power may be weaker than payrolls suggest.
“The unemployment rate for college graduates right now is like 5.6%.” ▶ 1:06:46
For the first time ever, Challenger Gray & Christmas data cites AI as the #1 reason for layoffs — and with AI deployment still in its earliest innings, Taggart warns the displacement effect compounds materially over the next 3-5 years.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: highAIChallenger Gray & Christmasjobstech layoffs
So what: Structural labor displacement is now measurable; a secular headwind for entry-level employment and consumer income.
“for the first time ever um AI is being given as the number one reason for layoffs”
Taggart and Roberts argue AI is eroding the bottom rungs of the career ladder across every profession (bookkeeping, entry-level work), removing the development onboarding humans need — a non-obvious structural risk distinct from whole-job automation.
thesis🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: highAIjobsbookkeeping
So what: Long-run productivity gains come with a hollowed-out talent pipeline; watch entry-level hiring as an AI-impact gauge.
“those rungs of the ladder are getting removed and so you can't go from off the ladder to rung three or four”
Portfolio trades & risk management 4
Roberts' RIA executed a value rotation: trimmed high-beta tech mid-day (Broadcom and other high-flyers) earlier, then added to beaten-down Walmart and Costco — citing Costco's membership-fee model as a durable cash-flow moat Walmart lacks.
anecdote🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highBroadcomWalmartCostco
So what: Concrete value-rotation playbook: take profits in stretched tech, add quality consumer-staples with structural moats.
“we added to Walmart. We added to Costco” ▶ 1:22:12
Additional RIA swaps within the value/dividend sleeve: sold Procter & Gamble for Coca-Cola, and swapped a ~2% Duke Energy position for a ~3% American Electric Power position — all favoring better relative performance among defensive dividend names.
anecdote🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumProcter & GambleCoca-ColaDuke EnergyAmerican Electric Power
So what: Shows active tilt toward dividend/defensive quality as the rotation plays out; specific name-level positioning.
“we actually uh sold Proctor Gamble and bought Coke”
Roberts' risk-management prescription for the correction: rebalance, cut high-beta exposure, add low-beta quality dividend stocks (terrible performers the last 3 years that protect on the downside), and add short-duration Treasuries (6-month to 1-year T-bills) to lower portfolio beta.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highTreasury billsquality stocksdividendsfixed income
So what: Actionable defensive allocation to ride out the pullback while staying invested.
“buy some, you know, six month, one-year Treasury bills. That'll help lower the beta volatility of your portfolio.”
On housing, Roberts says don't make a macro bet shorting subprime (don't 'be Michael Burry'); existing-home sales are at the lowest since the financial crisis and sellers are pulling listings, but a housing recession can occur without a systemic crisis or a 30% price crash.
contrarian🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumhousingMichael Burrysubprimereal estate
So what: Avoid the consensus housing-crash short; instead monitor and reduce portfolio risk if concerned, and shop distressed real estate selectively.
“don't be Michael Bur and go short subprime right now”
Today's Markets Are A Digital Casino On Cocaine | Chris Irons, Quoth The Raven75 min32 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
Chris Irons (Quoth the Raven) argues US equity markets are a 'permanently distorted' digital casino where the passive bid, 0DTE options gamma, and unlimited liquidity have killed fundamental active management, so he is exiting active trading and concentrating capital in gold, government bonds, emerging markets and asymmetric thematic bets (psychedelics, nuclear/SMR, cybersecurity) while warning the bond market is the key fault line that forces eventual yield-curve control.
Permanent distortion / market structure · 6Gamification of speculation · 3Macro: inflation, debt, dollar · 4Bond market as the fault line · 4Asymmetric thematic bets · 9Fundamentals vs. market reality · 3Tail risk and positioning posture · 3
Permanent distortion / market structure 6
Chris Irons argues historical valuation norms (market-cap-to-GDP, P/E) from 1920-2000 no longer apply because the Fed now prints money at every ~3% drawdown, creating a 'permanently distorted' market regime where old means are meaningless.
framework🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highS&P 500Federal ReserveShiller P/Emarket cap to GDP
So what: Valuation-based shorting or mean-reversion timing is structurally unreliable; size positions for a regime that can stay distorted far longer than fundamentals justify.
“those historical means, they don't mean anything now because in 1980, we weren't printing money at every 3% draw down in markets”
The S&P set four consecutive record highs last week with negative market breadth (more decliners than advancers) on all four days, evidence the index is driven by a handful of mega-cap names rather than broad participation.
data_point🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Index-level highs mask deteriorating breadth; a thinning leadership base is a classic late-cycle fragility signal to watch.
≈ “the S&P set four record highs and there were more decliners than advancers on all four days” paraphrase
Irons credits Mike Green's 'passive bid' thesis: market-cap-weighted index funds act as a price-insensitive 'giant mindless robot' buying at any valuation, which mechanically lets indices hit all-time highs even with negative breadth.
framework🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highMike Greenpassive fundsindex funds401k
So what: Inflows/outflows to passive vehicles, not fundamentals, are a primary price driver; watch fund-flow and 401k contribution dynamics for the marginal bid turning.
“Just buying at any valuation. And if you're doing that on a market weighted index, that's why you can have more decliners and advancers and set all-time highs.”
Irons describes a reflexive options mechanism where retail buying of short-dated options forces dealers/market-makers to buy the underlying stock, turning option flow into a self-reinforcing upward price driver (gamma squeeze).
causal🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highoptions marketRobinhood0DTE options
So what: Rallies powered by forced dealer hedging rather than conviction buying can reverse violently when the option flow flips; track gamma positioning as a stability gauge.
“if you buy enough of those options, the people writing them have are forced to go in and buy the stock”
Irons declares fundamental active management 'dead, at least for right now' — citing a billionaire friend who admitted 'I just don't know how to make money' ~8 years ago, marking the end of long/short investing on fundamentals.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highhedge fundsactive management
So what: Even identifying confirmed fraud or cheap cash-generative names earns no payoff if the market won't agree; fundamental conviction must be paired with flow/momentum awareness.
“investing on fundamentals sadly is dead at least for right now” ▶ 14:45
Irons argues the leverage and forced/gamma-driven nature of the rally means any selloff will be faster and more violent than historically, potentially gapping limit-down over a few days before the Fed intervenes.
prediction🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumS&P 500NASDAQFederal Reserve
So what: Tail-hedge and liquidity planning should assume a sharp non-linear drawdown rather than an orderly decline; the bounce-back catalyst is Fed action, not value buyers.
“due to that and leverage, it will snap lower a lot quicker”
Gamification of speculation 3
Irons frames modern markets as a 'digital casino on cocaine' — prediction markets, 24/7 crypto, pre-market options and sports betting on the same phone have collapsed the line between investing, entertainment and addiction.
framework🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highprediction marketscryptosports bettingoptions
So what: Ubiquitous speculation channels reflect and amplify froth; their proliferation is itself a late-cycle behavioral marker.
“it's a digital casino on cocaine because that is really what it's become”
Irons notes traders are now levered up to 500:1 trading single pips in crypto, equating it to betting balls-and-strikes in a baseball game — extreme retail leverage in always-on micro-speculation.
data_point🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumcrypto
So what: Extreme embedded leverage in retail crypto raises systemic fragility and forced-liquidation cascade risk during volatility spikes.
“now you got guys levered 500 to one trading single pips in crypto”
Irons advises 'be right and sit tight' (get it right, sit tight) — arguing that constant accessibility leads to over-trading and that for most participants the optimal action most of the time is to do nothing.
framework🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: high
So what: Behavioral discipline (fewer, higher-conviction decisions) is presented as the dominant edge in an environment engineered to provoke constant action.
“be right and sit tight” ▶ 22:38
Macro: inflation, debt, dollar 4
Irons cites a stressed macro backdrop: inflation ~4%, PPI ~6%, and credit-card and auto-loan delinquencies at, near, or above 2008 highs, set against record-high Shiller P/E and market-cap-to-GDP.
data_point🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highinflationPPIcredit cardsauto loansShiller P/E
So what: Simultaneous peak valuations and deteriorating consumer credit give the market the 'furthest to fall' it has ever had; monitor delinquency trends as a cycle tell.
“credit cards and autoloan delinquencies at or near or above 2008 highs”
Irons sees the US 'locked in a room with no exits': either the market crashes, the consumer runs out of money at ~3.5% rates, or the bond market forces a choice — and policymakers will pick the 'soft default' of more money printing and inflation rather than a hard default.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highFederal ReserveUS Treasurybond marketUS dollar
So what: Base case is debasement over default; positions hedge currency debasement (hard assets, non-dollar exposure) rather than a clean deflationary reset.
“We will take the soft default route which means printing more money and more inflation on top of inflation that we already have.”
Irons argues every round of money printing widens the wealth gap (top 1% gain faster than bottom 90%), a bipartisan policy — both Elizabeth Warren and Donald Trump are calling for lower rates — that will eventually drive social unrest.
causal🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highElizabeth WarrenDonald TrumpFederal ReserveBitcoin
So what: Persistent debasement is structurally embedded across both parties, reinforcing the secular case for inflation hedges and hard assets.
“Every time we print money, the wealth gap widens” ▶ 1:07:39
Irons flags de-dollarization risk: the Saudi petrodollar arrangement is eroding as oil is increasingly traded in yuan and gold, undermining the durability of US reserve-currency status despite the strong narrative.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumSaudi ArabiaUS dollaryuangoldAndy SchectmanBRICS
So what: Structural reserve-currency erosion supports gold and non-dollar allocations as a multi-year theme.
“now they're they're selling and trading oil in yuan and in gold”
Bond market as the fault line 4
Irons names the bond market — specifically the 10-year chart — as the single leading indicator he watches for when 'the wheels fall off,' citing 30-year yields near multi-decade highs around 5.15% and the 10-year near 5%.
framework🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: high10-year Treasury30-year Treasurybond market
So what: Watch long-end yields and weekly auctions as the primary trigger signal; a disorderly long end is the catalyst for the broader unwind.
“If I had to just watch one chart all day every day, it' probably be like the 10-year chart.”
Irons predicts the Fed will be forced into yield-curve control if the long end keeps malfunctioning, and warns the Fed is trapped between a deflationary depression and a hyperinflationary outcome at the next bout of market stress.
prediction🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highFederal Reservebond marketyield curve control
So what: YCC would suppress yields and accelerate currency debasement; position for financial repression and a steep negative real-rate environment.
“I think the obvious path for macro is the yield curve control”
Irons highlights bonds selling off alongside equities (rather than acting as a safe haven) as an emerging-market-style signal that investors are doubting US creditworthiness, and poses whether a war resolution with bonds still selling off would be the scariest tell.
contrarian🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumbond marketTreasuriesequities
So what: A broken stock-bond correlation removes the classic 60/40 hedge; rising yields amid risk-off would confirm a sovereign-credit repricing.
“that's like the kind of thing that you see in emerging market country where people are just doubting the creditworthiness of the country”
Irons believes that simply leaving rates at current ~3.5% levels would eventually dry up liquidity and drive consumer delinquencies higher, and that policymakers are already floating rate hikes this year.
causal🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumFederal Reserveinterest ratesconsumer credit
So what: Even a static-rate path is contractionary; the liquidity drain is the slow-burn mechanism that precedes the violent equity unwind.
Asymmetric thematic bets 9
Irons' annual watchlist of names outperformed the S&P by more than 50% last year and is beating it by more than 10% this year (equal-weighted), built around under-noticed, asymmetric ideas not covered by mainstream financial media.
data_point🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Track record supports his under-the-radar thematic approach; the edge is sourcing ideas before CNBC coverage rather than fundamental valuation.
≈ “Last year that list outperformed the S&P by like more than 50%. This year it's outperforming the S&P by more than 10%.” paraphrase
Irons is bullish on psychedelics as a multi-year asymmetric theme, expecting RFK Jr. as HHS Secretary and Trump executive orders to expedite FDA approvals (possibly as soon as late summer/early fall), leading to big-pharma buyouts of clinical-stage names.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highpsychedelicsPSI ETFCompass PathwaysRFK Jr.Donald TrumpFDAPfizeribogaine
So what: A regulatory-catalyst, binary-outcome theme uncorrelated to the economy; the PSI ETF and names like Compass Pathways are vehicles, but pre-revenue clinical risk is high.
≈ “I think with RFK in as Health and Human Services Secretary, and Trump signing the executive orders to kind of advance psychedelics through” paraphrase
Irons notes the PSI psychedelics ETF holds only ~$32M AUM (up from ~$20M), signaling the theme is still early and under-owned, and speculates it could run from ~$22 to $100-$150 if approvals and speculative fervor arrive.
prediction🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: exploratoryPSI ETFDRAM ETF
So what: Tiny AUM implies large upside torque if flows arrive, but also thin liquidity; sizing must respect the speculative, binary nature.
“it would not surprise me to see that ETF which trades at you know 22 go to 100 or go to 150”
Irons cites nuclear/small-modular-reactor (SMR) names as a theme he identified ~2.5 years early as an AI-energy offshoot, pointing to Oklo (tied to Sam Altman) running from ~$5 to ~$140 despite no meaningful revenue.
anecdote🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highnuclearSMROkloSam AltmanZero Hedge
So what: Demonstrates his 'story over fundamentals' playbook; nuclear/SMR remains tied to the AI power-demand narrative.
≈ “when names like Ollo came out... it was trading at you know 5 or something... lo and behold, it went to 140” paraphrase ▶ 52:00
Irons favors cybersecurity (via the IHAK ETF) as an under-owned theme, arguing future wars will be fought first through fiber optics and satellites before going 'hot,' and these names are trailing the broader AI trade.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumcybersecurityIHAK ETF
So what: Cybersecurity offers AI/defense exposure that has lagged the speculative AI names, a relative-value angle within the theme.
“the next few wars that we fight are going to be bought you know through fiber optic cable and and satellites first before they become hot wars”
Irons recommends getting AI exposure through cheap blue-chip proxies (Microsoft ~18x, Google, Amazon, Zoom ~12-15x) that already hold stakes in OpenAI/Anthropic, rather than paying ~20-40x NAV for private funds chasing Anthropic exposure.
contrarian🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highMicrosoftGoogleAmazonZoomOpenAIAnthropic
So what: A value-oriented backdoor into the AI theme; avoids the extreme premium and likely drawdown of private-fund AI vehicles.
≈ “Don't go out and pay 40 times NAV on some private fund that's going to get rocked.” paraphrase
Irons prefers a basket of emerging-market equities (EEM and Latin America via ILF) at P/Es of 10-20 over an S&P near ~35x at a bubble top, explicitly positioning longer gold, government bonds and emerging markets and less long US AI stocks/semiconductors.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highemerging marketsEEMILFgoldgovernment bondssemiconductorsS&P 500
So what: Concrete portfolio tilt: non-dollar, cheaper EM and hard assets over expensive US mega-cap tech as a debasement and valuation hedge.
≈ “would I rather own a basket of 20 countries... with price to earnings ratios between 10 and 20 than be overweight a market cap weighted S&P at whatever 35 times earnings at the top of a bubble?” paraphrase ▶ 52:48
Irons reminds listeners he called GDX (gold miners) going from ~$20 to ~$120 over two years, using it as evidence that once these under-noticed trends catch on, they move quickly.
anecdote🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highGDXgold minersgold
So what: Supports momentum-on-catalyst thesis for his current picks; gold-miner leverage to gold has already played out sharply.
“you're talking to the same guy who said the GDX was going to go to 100 when it was at 20 two years ago. And where did it go? Went to 120”
Irons favors consumer staples as a defensive rotation, having flagged them after two years of underperformance versus the S&P on the thesis that investors back into staples when they turn defensive.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumconsumer staples
So what: A defensive value rotation candidate if leadership narrows and risk appetite fades.
Fundamentals vs. market reality 3
Irons cites PayPal at ~9x earnings, gushing cash, buying back stock and owning Venmo as a name that 'should' be an obvious acquisition target, but argues it stays cheap unless private equity or the market chooses to put a bid under it.
anecdote🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumPayPalVenmoprivate equity
So what: Illustrates that cheap, high-quality names can stay cheap indefinitely without a flow catalyst — a value trap warning in the current regime.
≈ “Nine times earnings, gushing cash, buying back stock, got the number one financial asset, out there, I think, in Venmo.” paraphrase
Irons says a fundamentalist friend long cash-gushing 9x-earnings stocks and short the AI bubble is getting badly hurt this year, evidence that being fundamentally right offers no protection when the market ignores fundamentals.
anecdote🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highAI stocks
So what: Shorting the AI bubble on valuation alone has been a losing trade; timing and flow dominate fundamental correctness in this regime.
≈ “short the AI bubble right now and he's getting the kicked out of him this year” paraphrase
Irons warns there are companies trading at all-time highs that he believes are committing outright fraud (earnings from off-balance-sheet entities), but argues it is irrelevant because regulators and even courts lack the financial acumen to act.
contrarian🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highAndrew Leftshort sellers
So what: Fraud-based short theses lack a reliable catalyst given weak enforcement; the market not noticing is the binding constraint.
“You can point to a company say here's the fraud. the earnings are coming from this offbalance sheet entity. No one cares.”
Tail risk and positioning posture 3
Irons' top overall message is to take extraordinary caution, arguing the more euphoric and better things look, the more likely a violent reversal — invoking Bill Ackman's 'hell is coming' COVID call marking the bottom as evidence sentiment extremes work both ways.
contrarian🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highBill Ackman
So what: Treat extreme euphoria as a contrarian sell signal; raise caution and tail hedges as sentiment peaks.
“the more euphoric people become and the better things look, the more likely they are to snap violently in the other direction”
Irons argues markets were just a couple of days from real stress around 'liberation day,' which is why Bessent and Trump announced a 30-day tariff pause, implying policy intervention is the reflexive backstop near breaking points.
causal🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumScott BessentDonald Trumptariffs
So what: Policy puts trigger near liquidity breaking points; expect intervention to cap stress but not before sharp dislocations.
≈ “we were really just a couple of days from some real hitting the fan” paraphrase
Irons flags Japan's bond market as a parallel stress point, arguing that beyond printing money authorities have 'no real tools' to address a sovereign bond-market malfunction.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumJapanJGBsbond market
So what: Japan's bond market is a potential global contagion trigger; watch JGB yields alongside US long-end stress.
“I don't know what's going to happen in Japan with their bond market right now”
DEBATE: Does The Entire World Face A "Bronze Age Type" Collapse? | Brent Johnson vs Craig Tindale73 min36 insights2026-05-31
▶ Watch on YouTube
A macro debate between Brent Johnson (Dollar Milkshake) and Craig Tindale on whether the West cedes hegemony to China. Tindale argues a financialized West has lost the material/industrial economy to China and that physical resource limits (copper, rare earths, fertilizer, electricity) make the AI/data-center buildout and current growth unsustainable for both sides — a 'Bronze Age type' collapse. Johnson counters the US won't cede dominance, that the transition triggers a stronger dollar, liquidity/sovereign-debt crises, and that the real victims are peripheral emerging markets, not the two great powers. Investment takeaway: rotate from financial assets into hard assets/commodities, refiners, agricultural commodities and re-industrialization plays; be wary of AI/data-center names hitting energy and material bottlenecks.
Financial vs material economy bifurcation (the Tindale Trap) · 4China supply-chain dependency and the West's deindustrialization · 4Currency regime change — Dollar Milkshake, CNY transactional rise, and the death of small currencies · 5Physical limits to growth — the 'Bronze Age collapse' thesis · 5AI / data-center energy and equipment bottlenecks · 4Sovereign-debt / liquidity crisis cascade and the geography of pain · 4US strategic advantages and the 'ring of power' reserve currency · 4Actionable investment themes — hard assets, agriculture, and aviation MRO · 6
Financial vs material economy bifurcation (the Tindale Trap) 4
Tindale's core framework ('the Tindale Trap') is a bifurcation between a financialized paper economy and a material/industrial economy, and the West has reached an endpoint where the material economy no longer responds to financial-economy stimulus as it did over the prior 40-50 years.
framework🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highUnited StatesChinaFederal Reserve
So what: Implies monetary easing/liquidity will increasingly fail to produce real output in the West, favoring hard assets over financial assets as the policy transmission mechanism breaks.
“it's a bifocation between a financial economy and a material economy”
The US financial economy is roughly $400 trillion, of which Tindale estimates only ~1% is metals, chemicals and refined products, a ratio he argues must mean-revert toward the industrial economy.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highUnited Statescommoditiesmetalschemicals
So what: Frames a multi-year rotation thesis: capital flowing out of paper assets into physical/industrial assets as the 'table turns.'
“The financial economy in the US is about $400 trillion... 1% of that at the moment is metals and chemicals and and refined products” ▶ 1:00:14
Tindale argues the financial economy has grown from roughly 8% to about 23% of the economy while Main Street de-industrialized, driven by FOMC/Fed policy acting like a 'super lobby group' that channeled wages into financial products and inflated assets via Bernanke's wealth effect.
causal🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highFederal ReserveFOMCBen Bernankehousingequities
So what: Positions Fed-driven financialization as structurally fragile and reversing, a tailwind for real assets and a headwind for inflated financial valuations.
“we've we've seen the financial economy grow from I think 8% and I think it's about 23%”
Tindale's investment posture is to cycle his own capital out of the financial economy and into the industrial economy, treating the rotation as the dominant multi-decade theme of his book 'The Return to Matter.'
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highindustrial economycommodities
So what: A clear actionable allocation signal: overweight industrial/material exposure, underweight purely financial assets.
“that's where I'm putting all all my money that that that uh that cycle into the industrial economy and out of the financial economy”
China supply-chain dependency and the West's deindustrialization 4
China supplies an estimated 50-98% of metals and most refined industrial inputs to the West, and the US cannot independently source titanium for F-35s or copper for data centers without Chinese permission, constituting a critical national-security dependency.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highChinatitaniumcopperrare earthsF-35data centers
So what: Re-shoring of refining and component manufacturing becomes a strategic imperative, favoring Western miners, refiners and material-tech companies addressing these dependencies.
“it can't get its titanium for the F-35s it can't get... its copper for its data centers” ▶ 4:40
India is as dependent on China as the US is, sourcing 100% of its rare earths for defense products from China, per a forthcoming Tindale essay — the supply-chain vulnerability is not US-specific.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highIndiaChinarare earthsdefense
So what: Broadens the rare-earth supply-security trade beyond the US to a global re-sourcing scramble, supporting non-Chinese rare-earth supply.
“India um you know gets 100% of its uh rare earths for defense products and... just about everything else from China”
The US gave away manufacturing to China expecting China to Westernize; that bet failed, and Johnson and Tindale agree the West now must rebuild manufacturing capacity it 'forgot how to do,' from refining to component production.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUnited StatesChinamanufacturing
So what: Underwrites a structural re-industrialization capex cycle in the West — supportive of domestic industrial, refining and capital-goods plays.
“the United States gave away their manufacturing prowess to China in the hopes that China would become more western... that did not happen”
Commodity exporters like Australia sell raw materials but not refined end-products, leaving them structurally akin to a factory that ships inputs to someone else (China) for value-added refining — a vulnerability for resource-exporter currencies and economies.
causal🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highAustraliaBHPChinacommodities
So what: Suggests value-capture, and ultimately currency strength, accrues to refiners/end-producers (China) over raw-material exporters, a structural headwind for AUD-style commodity economies.
“it's akin to having a factory that um takes all the raw materials, but doesn't produce the end product”
Currency regime change — Dollar Milkshake, CNY transactional rise, and the death of small currencies 5
Tindale predicts a regime shift from a single USD reserve currency to a 'block currency' structure: the USD becomes the debt-servicing and allied-block currency (~56-58% of global debt is USD-denominated) while the CNY becomes the transactional currency for buying physical commodities like copper and rare earths that China refines.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highUS dollarChinese yuanCNYcopperrare earths
So what: Implies bifurcated FX demand: persistent USD demand for debt service even as CNY gains share in commodity settlement — watch CNY commodity-pricing milestones as a structural signal.
“the the currency that's needed to buy copper... is basically CNY... uh or rare earth because... they do most of the refining” ▶ 9:07
BHP, which had always insisted on US dollars, is now accepting CNY — cited as concrete evidence that the commodity-settlement currency is shifting toward the yuan.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highBHPCNYUS dollar
So what: A trackable data point: commodity-major acceptance of CNY is an early marker of de-dollarization in trade settlement even as USD dominates debt.
“you got examples like BHP who had always insisted on US dollars now taking CNY”
Johnson reiterates the Dollar Milkshake thesis: during the chaotic transition to a new monetary system, the dollar rises versus other fiat currencies (not necessarily versus real things like energy/commodities), and that rise triggers credit contraction, liquidity crises, currency crises and sovereign failures.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUS dollarfiat currenciessovereign bonds
So what: A long-USD-vs-fiat positioning with the expectation that dollar strength is the trigger for global liquidity/credit stress — relevant for hedging EM and peripheral-sovereign exposure.
“I expect the dollar to rise not fall... when the dollar rises versus other fiat currencies, bad things happen in markets” ▶ 22:56
Tindale predicts the end of many small/peripheral currencies rather than the end of the USD or CNY — weak currencies get absorbed into a USD-allied block via dollar swap lines (e.g. the UAE), which Tindale expects to effectively become the block's local-support and transfer mechanism.
prediction🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highUS dollarCNYUAEdollar swapsScott Bessent
So what: Implies short small-EM-currency / consolidation risk; dollar-swap inclusion becomes a survival marker for smaller economies and a tool the US Treasury wields.
“I'm not predicting the end of the USD or the CNY. I'm predicting the end of probably a lot of the others”
Both debaters expect continued dollarization (not de-dollarization) within the US-allied block, while a competing China-centric block may also coalesce, with stablecoins playing a role in extending USD reach.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumUS dollarstablecoinsChina
So what: Supports exposure to USD-linked rails (incl. stablecoins) as a vehicle of dollar entrenchment rather than betting on near-term reserve-currency replacement.
Physical limits to growth — the 'Bronze Age collapse' thesis 5
Tindale's central contrarian thesis is that this is not a US-vs-China baton change but a third outcome — a 'Bronze Age type' collapse — because global physical production capacity for key materials is insufficient for either side to build what it wants, echoing the 1973 Limits to Growth study.
contrarian🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highcopperrare earthsLimits to Growth
So what: Reframes the macro debate from geopolitical winner-take-all to a supply-constrained world where scarce real resources outperform regardless of which power 'wins.'
“I think we we're we're facing a much more fundamental bronze age type uh collapse where the things that we want to do um aren't aren't sustainable”
Tindale quantifies the copper shortfall: the world needs to open about six copper mines per year to meet demand but is opening roughly one, a structural supply deficit independent of the geopolitical rivalry.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highcoppermining
So what: A specific, durable bull case for copper prices and copper miners driven by chronic under-investment in new supply.
“we have to open six copper mines a year and refine the copper and we're opening one”
Tantalum demand is projected at roughly five times current production within five years, illustrating acute looming shortfalls across multiple critical minerals beyond copper.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: hightantalumcritical minerals
So what: Points to severe pricing/scarcity for specialty minerals like tantalum — a niche supply-security trade.
“tantelum, for instance, we need five times more tantelum in five years than we we currently produce”
Tindale frames China and the US as 'conjoined twins choking each other' in an integrated economy where China still imports ~$2.5 trillion of goods, so a successful decoupling damages both — neither power cleanly wins.
framework🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highChinaUnited States
So what: Cautions against one-sided 'China rises / West falls' or 'West wins' trades; favors assets that benefit from mutual stress (real assets, scarcity) over directional power bets.
“I compare them to... conjoined twins who are trying to choke each other that if successful they both die” ▶ 20:43
Tindale argues re-industrialization is rate-limited by physics, analogizing it to a three-legged race between Gordon Moore (bits, fast) and Einstein (atoms, physically constrained) — the material economy simply cannot scale at software speed.
framework🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highsemiconductorsindustrial economy
So what: Tempers expectations for how fast Western re-shoring and AI hardware buildout can occur; bottlenecks persist and reward incumbents/holders of scarce physical capacity.
“Gordon can go a lot faster because he's bits and bites, but Einstein can't go fast at all because he's... constrained by physics”
AI / data-center energy and equipment bottlenecks 4
Tindale is bearish on AI companies, arguing the data-center buildout collides with physical limits — citing a claim attributed to Jensen Huang that AI needs ~1,000x more electricity than is currently produced, which Tindale calls absurd and unmet.
contrarian🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highAIdata centersJensen HuangNvidiaelectricity
So what: A short/avoid signal on richly valued AI names whose growth assumptions ignore power and material constraints; favors power/grid and energy beneficiaries instead.
“Jensen who said it, we need a thousand times more electricity... than we're currently producing. Now, that's ridiculous” ▶ 20:23
Tindale cites Elon Musk warning that the AI industry will run out of electricity by year-end, an under-noticed comment underscoring near-term power scarcity as a constraint on AI growth.
prediction🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: mediumElon MuskAIelectricity
So what: Reinforces a near-term electricity-bottleneck thesis: power generation/grid capacity becomes the binding constraint and the place to be positioned.
“Elon Musk... said that everyone's going to run out of electricity in the AI industry... by the end of the year”
Grid transformers from Siemens have a five-to-six-year lead time against a reported 148 billion euro backlog, and sulfuric-acid furnaces ('crackers') are similarly unobtainable on short notice — concrete equipment log-jams throttling electrification and refining.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highSiemenstransformersgridsulfuric acid
So what: Identifies electrical-equipment and specialty-process-equipment makers (transformers, furnaces) as supply-constrained beneficiaries with multi-year backlogs and pricing power.
“you can't get transformers from Seammens for five to six years. They got 148 billion euro back order”
Tindale predicts AI will be culturally elevated to a quasi-deity and 'independent' authority (analogous to Fed independence), with governments handing more decisions to it as a control mechanism amid chaos — a managerial/technocratic overreach he ties to Trumpism as backlash and to surveillance states like Britain.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: mediumAIFederal ReservePalantirAlex KarpUnited Kingdom
So what: Implies durable demand for AI-enabled state surveillance/control software (e.g. Palantir-type) even as broad AI valuations face material constraints — a bifurcated AI view.
Sovereign-debt / liquidity crisis cascade and the geography of pain 4
Johnson argues the supply-chain and resource problems Tindale identifies ultimately express as a currency crisis and sovereign-debt crisis — but primarily for Southeast Asian, peripheral European and some developed economies, not for the US or China.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highsovereign debtSoutheast AsiaEuropeUS dollar
So what: Directs the crisis-hedge toward peripheral sovereigns/EM rather than the core; the two great powers are expected to weather it best.
“a lot of the things that Craig has very correctly pointed out end up being a currency crisis and a sovereign debt crisis. perhaps not for the United States and China”
Johnson describes a self-reinforcing doom loop: small import-dependent countries must print local currency to buy USD-priced energy and food, which depreciates their currency further and spikes their sovereign yields, accelerating toward default.
causal🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highemerging marketsUS dollarenergyfoodsovereign bonds
So what: A mechanism for shorting/avoiding vulnerable EM sovereign debt and currencies as commodity prices rise; rising USD-priced inputs are the trigger.
“they have to then print their own currency... that ends up making the their currency fall even more... that causes their yields to jump on their their sovereign bonds” ▶ 29:16
Johnson explicitly times the potential sovereign-debt/currency crisis as anywhere from 9-12 months to 9-12 years out, declining to pin timing but stating he understands the 'plumbing' and that the transition cannot happen without great chaos.
prediction🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumsovereign debtcurrencies
So what: Wide time horizon argues for maintaining the hedge structurally rather than trading a specific catalyst date.
“I could make a case that it gets there in the next 9 to 12 months, but... I could make the case it'll take another nine to 12 years as well”
Tindale identifies the most disadvantaged economies as input-import-dependent developing nations (Bangladesh, India, Thailand and other Southeast Asian states) lacking fertilizer, LNG, herbicides and petrochemicals — not the slow-growth developed world per se.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highBangladeshIndiaThailandfertilizerLNGpetrochemicals
So what: Pinpoints input-deficit EMs as the locus of social/political instability and currency stress — relevant for EM risk screening.
US strategic advantages and the 'ring of power' reserve currency 4
Johnson argues the US holding the global reserve currency is like holding 'the ring of power' — an immense weapon usable against opponents — and that under Treasury Secretary Scott Bessant the system is shifting from a rules-based / greater-good (Keynesian) order to an 'America First' deployment of the monetary system.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUS dollarScott BessantUS TreasuryFederal Reserve
So what: Signals a more weaponized, self-interested US monetary policy with large external spillovers — bullish USD leverage, bearish for those on the wrong side of US policy.
“the United States has the global reserve currency, it is still like having the ring of power”
Asked which country to pick in a game of Risk, Johnson chooses the US for its aggregate geostrategic advantages (military, resources, reserve currency), while Tindale picks Australia, citing his own re-modeled climate scenarios showing Australia/New Zealand remaining 'wet, hot but quite survivable' versus worse outcomes elsewhere.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUnited StatesAustraliaNew Zealandclimate
So what: Both converge that the resource-rich, militarily strong powers (US, China, Australia) survive best — relevant for geographic capital-safety and agricultural-land theses.
“to to me there's no question you pick the United States” ▶ 50:07
Tindale points to a strengthening US-allied block — a recently signed Quad-style agreement among Japan, the US and Australia, plus Asian states (Philippines, Korea, Japan) seeking a balance against China — as evidence the West is actively, and fairly competently, fighting back.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: mediumQuadJapanUnited StatesAustraliaPhilippinesSouth KoreaChina
So what: An allied economic/security block is coalescing — supportive of intra-block trade, defense and supply-chain integration plays.
Tindale credits the Trump administration's economic team — Bessant at Treasury and Chris Wright on energy — as 'fairly smart' and evidence the US republic 'has a lot of fight left in it,' pushing back on naive 'China simply rises, America fades' narratives.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: mediumDonald TrumpScott BessantChris WrightUnited States
So what: Tempers outright bearish-US positioning; policy competence on currency and energy is a partial offset to structural decline.
Actionable investment themes — hard assets, agriculture, and aviation MRO 6
Tindale's actionable allocation is commodities, miners and refiners — but selectively, warning that many commodities depend on choke-point inputs like sulfuric acid used in leach mining of copper, nickel and rare earths, so miner/refiner selection must account for input dependencies.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highcoppernickelrare earthssulfuric acidminersrefiners
So what: Buy commodity producers but screen for input-supply security (sulfuric acid, energy) rather than buying the sector indiscriminately.
“some of the commodities are beholden to things like sulfuric acid... leech... mining is is one of the first... stages of of a lot of copper mining”
Tindale flags copper and gold as 'no-brainers' and highlights an emerging cohort of Western material-technology companies building domestic refining/production capacity — including an unnamed titanium company he expects to become the US titanium provider.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highcoppergoldtitaniummaterial technology
So what: Points to onshore critical-material producers (titanium, refining tech) as a growth subsector benefiting from supply-security re-shoring.
“I think we're going to be anchored in the material roots of the economy”
Johnson favors agricultural commodities — soybean meal, corn, wheat — on asymmetric risk/reward (limited downside, large upside) and is genuinely worried about a 2026 food shortage driven by fertilizer shortfalls (Strait of Hormuz disruption), high energy prices and a near-record El Nino.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highsoybean mealcornwheatfertilizerEl NinoStrait of Hormuz
So what: A specific long-grains/agriculture trade with favorable convexity tied to fertilizer and energy supply shocks.
“I think we have an opportunity that they're not going to go down a whole lot... but they have the potential to go up quite a bit”
Johnson flags a non-obvious trade in aviation MRO (maintenance, repair and overhaul): a years-long pre-existing backlog for widebody aircraft concentrated in Abu Dhabi, compounded by Middle East disruption, could force aircraft groundings and a supply constraint showing up in roughly nine months even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens tomorrow.
prediction🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumaviationMROwidebody aircraftAbu DhabiStrait of Hormuz
So what: An under-followed bottleneck idea: aviation MRO capacity and related names could benefit from grounding-driven scarcity in global air freight/travel late 2026 into 2027.
“there's an industry called MRO which stands for maintenance, repair and overhaul of airplanes... one of the major places where this takes place for widebody aircraft... Abu Dhabi”
Johnson cautions against over-bearishness and apocalypticism, arguing there are 'incredible opportunities' alongside the challenges and that investors should not 'check out, move to a mountain top, and come back in 10 years.'
framework🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highmarkets
So what: A balance check: stay invested and opportunistic in real assets rather than going fully defensive/cash on the dark macro thesis.
“there's incredible opportunities, too. And I think there is a danger in being overly bearish and overly apocryphal”
Johnson singles out Tindale's Substack report on 'the importance of Japan to the United States' as essential reading, and both name Japan and Korea as pivotal to the US over the next few years — flagging Japan as an under-appreciated macro focus.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumJapanSouth KoreaUnited States
So what: Signals Japan/Korea as a macro theme to research for allied-block supply-chain and defense positioning.
SPECIAL REPORT: Will The Fertilizer Shortage Create A Global Food Crisis? | Bruce Sherrick63 min38 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
University of Illinois land economist Bruce Sherrick argues the Strait of Hormuz closure and tariffs delivered a fertilizer/energy PRICE shock, not a physical supply shock, for the northern hemisphere this harvest — most fertilizer was already in the ground — so he is NOT ringing a global-famine alarm for fall 2026. His real worries are: a prolonged conflict spilling into a wider regional war, food-price-driven insecurity in Africa and western parts of Asia, and depressed US commodity prices from trade rerouting. His central investment thesis is farmland as the most-fixed, inflation-hedging, equity-uncorrelated asset poised for a structural demand wave from the $126T boomer wealth transfer.
Hormuz/Tariff shock is price, not physical supply (this harvest) · 5Fertilizer supply mechanics, dependency, and inelasticity · 5Geopolitics, escalation risk, and the food-security red line · 6Trade rerouting and depressed US commodity prices · 4Second-order effects: steel, equipment, processing chain · 4Named companies and episodic fertilizer trade · 4Farmland as the structural inflation-hedge asset · 7Ag-tech productivity revolution · 3
Hormuz/Tariff shock is price, not physical supply (this harvest) 5
The Strait of Hormuz closure, through which roughly one-third of the world's seaborne fertilizer trade passes, will NOT cause a northern-hemisphere fertilizer shortage for the 2026 fall harvest because most of the required fertilizer had already shipped and was applied before the closure (a 'pig in the python' lag).
thesis🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrick (U. Illinois land economist)conviction: highStrait of Hormuzfertilizernorthern hemisphereIran
So what: Fades panic-driven longs in fertilizer/ag on a 'famine this fall' narrative; the actionable shock is price/margin, not tonnage availability, for the current cycle.
“it's sort of a pig in the python problem. It takes a long time to work something through anyhow and most of the fertilizer was already in place for the northern hemisphere planting season this year”
Much of the US corn crop's nitrogen was applied last fall as anhydrous ammonia and most dry fertilizers were already contracted and in place before spring, so 2026 northern-hemisphere planting was largely insulated from the Hormuz disruption.
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highnitrogenanhydrous ammoniacornUnited States
So what: The window for a physical-shortage trade in 2026 northern-hemisphere inputs has effectively closed; watch the NEXT application cycle (fall 2026 / southern-hemisphere) instead.
“much of the nitrogen application that was required for the corn crop in the US was done last fall”
Fertilizer prices rose sharply from a combination of the Hormuz closure, Canada/Mexico tariffs that pre-dated the closure, and higher energy prices that feed directly into ammonia production — but unlike the 2022 Russia-Ukraine shock there was NO offsetting rise in output/commodity prices.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highfertilizerCanadaMexicotariffsnatural gasammonia
So what: Pure input-cost squeeze on farmer margins with no commodity-price tailwind — bearish for US grower net income and a margin headwind for grain-dependent processors.
“the net effect is that the input prices went up quite a bit. There's not a real material response on what you might think of as the output prices”
In 2022 the Russia-Ukraine war raised input costs but also lifted commodity prices (especially wheat, sympathetically corn and soybeans) because Black Sea grain exports were cut; the Hormuz case is different because virtually no commodities flow through Hormuz, only energy and fertilizer.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highRussiaUkraineBlack SeawheatcornsoybeansStrait of Hormuz
So what: Don't model the Hormuz event off the 2022 playbook — there is no automatic grain-price hedge for farmers this time, so the net P&L effect on growers is worse.
Higher energy prices transmit into agriculture faster and more directly than fertilizer prices, with diesel costs described as 'really really high right now,' so energy should always be paired with fertilizer when assessing the food-cost shock.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highdieselenergynatural gasfertilizer
So what: Energy is the faster-moving channel into food costs; oil/diesel price relief would ease ag input inflation more quickly than fertilizer relief.
“whenever we talk about fertilizer we should continue to say and energy because diesel costs are really really high right now as well”
Fertilizer supply mechanics, dependency, and inelasticity 5
The US imports only ~13% of its nitrogen and ~16% of its phosphate, whereas Brazil imports ~95% of nitrogen and ~72% of phosphate — and BOTH countries import essentially 100% of their potassium (potash).
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highnitrogenphosphatepotassiumpotashUnited StatesBrazil
So what: Brazil is far more exposed to a fertilizer trade disruption than the US; potash is the universal import-dependency choke point for both — watch potash producers and Brazilian ag input costs.
“The US imports about 13% of its nitrogen. Brazil imports about 95%”
Fertilizer supply is highly inelastic: a new large-scale plant is a multi-year project, existing plants run near full utilization (not at 60% spare capacity), and nitrogen in particular is a near-real-time system locked to seasonal application needs.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highfertilizernitrogenammonia
So what: Supply cannot ramp to backfill interrupted shipments quickly, so any prolonged disruption is a durable price tailwind for incumbent producers with available capacity.
“you can't just put in a large scale fertilizer plant in a you know, that's not even a one-year project. That's a big project”
Mined nutrients (phosphate, potash — 'dig it out of the ground and grind it up') have somewhat more flexible production than nitrogen, but nitrogen output is essentially fixed and cannot be ramped dramatically.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumphosphatepotashnitrogen
So what: Differentiates producers: mined-nutrient miners have marginally more flex; nitrogen producers benefit most from price spikes since supply is rigid.
Russian fertilizer/ammonia supply out of the Black Sea is partially but not fully restored — some ammonia pipelines and transmission channels were destroyed and have not been rebuilt, and the condition of Odessa's deep-water ports is uncertain.
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumRussiaUkraineBlack SeaOdessaammonia
So what: A persistent structural reduction in global ammonia/fertilizer export capacity supports a higher baseline price floor than pre-2022.
“there were a couple of ammonia um pipelines that have not been restored”
Rationing and crop-mix shifts are the likely adjustment to tight fertilizer: US growers tend to over-apply nitrogen for a perfect year, and soybeans (a nitrogen-fixing legume) need far less nitrogen, so a squeeze pushes acreage toward soybeans and lighter input crops.
prediction🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumnitrogensoybeanscornacreage
So what: A fertilizer squeeze is a relative bid for soybean over corn acreage — watch the corn/soybean acreage and price ratio as a fertilizer-stress indicator.
Geopolitics, escalation risk, and the food-security red line 6
Sherrick's biggest red line is not the next crop failing but the conflict escalating beyond a 2-3 party fight: as soon as it spills past US-only headlines into a broader regional war, he becomes 'really really worried' about a prolonged bloody conflict that disrupts energy and food.
thesis🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumIranMiddle EastStrait of Hormuzenergy
So what: The tail risk to monitor is conflict widening (Gulf oil exporters drawn in), not a single bad harvest — escalation headlines are the trigger to re-risk ag/energy/food trades.
≈ “as soon as it spills out of the the US being the only headlines that we hear about day to day, then I am really really worried” paraphrase
If the Hormuz closure persists another month or two it would materially impact the SOUTHERN hemisphere's next planting cycle (~6 months out), making that the next real shortage-risk window rather than the current northern-hemisphere harvest.
prediction🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumStrait of Hormuzsouthern hemisphereBrazilArgentina
So what: Sets a concrete monitoring clock: a multi-month Hormuz closure is the catalyst for southern-hemisphere (Brazil/Argentina) input and crop risk in the following cycle.
“If it goes on another month or two, then it begins to really really impact southern hemisphere's next round of planting”
Real food-shortage pockets are emerging for the first time in a long time in parts of Africa and the western part of Asia that depend on imported food — driven by both physical scarcity and unaffordable prices ('you can starve people with prices just the same way you can starve people with lack of food').
prediction🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumAfricaAsiafood securityfood inflation
So what: Localized food-insecurity / social-unrest risk (the 'Arab Spring' analog) is concentrated in import-dependent emerging markets, not developed economies — an EM political-risk signal.
“you can starve people with prices just the same way you can starve people with lack of food”
For roughly a third of the world's population, a marginal dollar of income changes what they eat tomorrow, whereas in the US food is a low enough share of income that food inflation is barely noticed despite being a big component of the recent 3.8% inflation print.
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highfood inflationCPIemerging markets
So what: Food inflation's social/political impact is asymmetric and concentrated in poor importing nations — a leading indicator for EM instability, not for US consumer behavior.
“For about a third of the world's population, if you gave them each another dollar, they might change what they eat tomorrow”
Iran has already demonstrated willingness to destroy energy infrastructure (including natural gas) of neighbors, and because everything in food production depends on energy, attacks that raise energy costs erode the value/affordability of all downstream food inputs.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumIrannatural gasenergy infrastructureammonia
So what: Energy-infrastructure attacks are the transmission mechanism from geopolitics to food costs (natural gas to ammonia) — a key escalation channel to watch.
Even if the war ended and Hormuz reopened tomorrow, productivity and output would not snap back to the prior path — like a temporary smoker, the system permanently loses some of the gains and resources wasted during the disruption.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumproductivitysupply chains
So what: Reopening is not a full reversal — there's a permanent output 'gap' left on the table, so a peace-deal relief rally may overstate the recovery.
“you don't go all the way back up to the same line” ▶ 37:59
Trade rerouting and depressed US commodity prices 4
Tariffs and geopolitical conflict have already begun 'unspinning' global ag trade — e.g., China buying soybeans from Argentina/Brazil instead of the US, forcing US exporters to backfill more distant markets at much higher transportation cost borne by the seller.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highChinaArgentinaBrazilsoybeansUnited Statestariffs
So what: Structurally bearish for US soybean prices and grower margins; bullish relative positioning for South American exporters — a durable trade-reordering theme.
“when China decides to buy from Argentina and Brazil and not from the US and the US has to then go fill in backfill the rest of the world demand for soybeans to places it didn't used to sell”
Lower US commodity prices are likely to PERSIST even if China meets soybean-purchase obligations under trade agreements, because the expensive rerouting of global trade is paid for largely by the seller.
prediction🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highUnited StatesChinasoybeanscommodity prices
So what: Lean bearish on US grain prices/grower income into the trade-reordering regime even on headline 'China is buying' news.
“lower prices are likely to persist in the US” ▶ 17:42
Sherrick is as worried about depressed US output prices from trade rerouting as he is about next year's crop physically not being produced, since output prices ultimately drive planting decisions.
thesis🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highcommodity pricesUnited Statesplanting
So what: Reframes the dominant risk from supply-side famine to demand/price-side margin compression for US producers.
“So I'm as worried about that as I am about next year's crop not being able to be produced”
Comparative advantage means the US cannot economically replace interrupted fertilizer supply just because it has cheap, sometimes-flared natural gas — artificial trade barriers (tariffs) are 'really sloppy taxing' and natural gas is most valuable in its current uses.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highnatural gasammoniacomparative advantagetariffs
So what: Skeptical of a US fertilizer-onshoring thesis as an economic winner; tariffs raise system-wide costs rather than create durable domestic advantage.
“it's just really sloppy taxing” ▶ 21:41
Second-order effects: steel, equipment, processing chain 4
Steel and aluminum tariffs may matter more to farm economics over time than fertilizer prices because they permanently raise the cost of durable equipment — a combine that cost ~$1 million could cost ~$1.2 million, and that elevated cost lasts the machine's entire useful life.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highsteelaluminumtariffscombineJohn Deerefarm equipment
So what: A long-duration, under-appreciated cost drag on farm capex and margins — bearish for farmer economics and a margin/pricing variable for equipment makers like John Deere.
“a combine that used to cost a million might be a million too because of the increased cost of components”
Wheat itself is only ~10-15% of the cost of a loaf of bread, with transportation and packaging each a similar share, so the more processed a food and the slower the economy turns, the more all energy-linked input costs compound into the final consumer price.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highwheatbreadfood inflationpackagingtransportation
So what: Food-price inflation is driven more by energy-linked processing/transport/packaging than by raw grain prices — track energy and logistics, not just crop futures, for consumer food CPI.
“If you take wheat and say per bushel how much of the you know value of a bread a loaf of bread is represented by the input of wheat it's a really small fraction 10 15% maybe”
Plastics and other oil-derived inputs (for wrapping, cooling, freezing) mean the food system's energy exposure runs far beyond diesel — the most-processed foods carry the highest embedded energy cost and erode most when energy prices rise.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumplasticsoilenergyfood processing
So what: Highly-processed/packaged food categories are most exposed to energy-cost inflation — relevant for packaged-food company margins.
A smaller US beef herd will keep beef prices elevated until the herd is rebuilt, a dynamic largely separate from the fertilizer/energy shock but additive to food-price stickiness.
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highbeefcattle herd
So what: Beef prices stay structurally high on herd-cycle dynamics independent of the Hormuz event — a separate persistent food-inflation component.
“we have a smaller beef herd right now. So beef prices will stay up until we have a bigger beef herd”
Named companies and episodic fertilizer trade 4
