So what: Forward PE returns are at risk as cheap entry points vanish and exit-to-public/strategic buyers dries up; the model needs the public market to keep absorbing flips.
“one day you wake up and you realize you're the last private equity guy rolling up the last HVAC company”
The bull case and sentiment divergence 4
The clearest bull case is that fiscal deficits create private-sector credit/earnings: Trump has pushed the rest of the world (Europe, Japan) to spend, so monstrous aggregate global deficits keep feeding corporate earnings until capital actually runs out.
thesis🗣 Kevin Muirconviction: mediumfiscal deficitTrumpglobal stimulusS&P 500 earningsprivate sector credit
So what: As long as global deficits run large, the earnings tailwind persists — fade the deficit-driven rally only when capital constraints actually bind.
“fiscal deficits create private sector credits” ▶ 1:00:15
Financial repression (Russell Napier-style yield curve control) would force capital into equities by removing alternatives, providing a structural bid even at stretched valuations.
prediction🗣 Kevin Muirconviction: mediumfinancial repressionRussell Napieryield curve controlequities
So what: If YCC arrives, the scarcity of acceptable destinations for capital pushes money into stocks regardless of valuation — a tail-bull scenario.
Corporations have learned to manage not just earnings beats (~80% beat rate via guided bars) but the entire investor narrative, letting markets 'defy gravity' and stay elevated far longer than bears expect outside an actual economic contraction.
thesis🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumcorporate earningsearnings guidanceBen Huntnarrative
So what: Narrative management argues against going all-cash; keep some equity exposure because the 'don't look down' dynamic can persist.
“the narrative of not looking down could make us defy gravity for longer than people expect”
There is a striking sentiment divergence: allocators' actual clients ('civilians') are optimistic while macro-podcast audiences are jarringly bearish, suggesting bearishness is concentrated in a self-selected commentariat rather than real money.
data_point🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumsentimentretail investorspodcast audiences
So what: Contrarian read: extreme online bearishness may not reflect positioning of actual clients, weakening the 'everyone is bearish so we crash' setup.
“my clients are optimistic. Yeah. It's the podcast listeners who are not. And it's jarring how wide that spread is right now.”

80,000 Hours Podcast 1 episode(s) · 34 insights

I lead AGI safety at Google DeepMind – here's the view from the inside | Rohin Shah168 min34 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
Rohin Shah, head of AGI alignment & safety at Google DeepMind, lays out an unusually concrete insider view: AI progress is continuous/forecastable (no abrupt capability jumps), an intelligence explosion is coming but is years not months away and his own AGI timeline is ~a decade, chain-of-thought monitoring stays useful ~4-5 years for structural GPU/transformer reasons, and prosaic alignment plus third-party auditing (not binding commitments or pre-deployment eval mandates) is the right safety path. Rich with second-order signal on compute/inference scaling, DeepMind vs OpenAI/Anthropic positioning, and what becomes the safety bottleneck (implementation/software engineering, not ideas).
AGI timelines & intelligence-explosion mechanics · 5AI progress is continuous & measurable (no abrupt jumps) · 4Compute, inference scaling & the cost curve · 3DeepMind vs OpenAI/Anthropic positioning · 4Safety → regulation: auditing over commitments & eval mandates · 6Misalignment risk: lower than headlines, but real · 3Chain-of-thought monitoring & technical safety bets · 4Governance gap & second-order societal effects · 3Labor, hiring & the AI-application deluge · 2
AGI timelines & intelligence-explosion mechanics 5
Rohin Shah's median timeline to AI systems that can automate most AI R&D is roughly a decade, which he notes would once have been called 'extremely short' but is now framed as 'medium to long' — a longer view than the current AGI-2027/2030 consensus among AI-safety insiders.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shah, head of AGI alignment & safety at Google DeepMindconviction: highGoogle DeepMindAGI
So what: A senior DeepMind insider pushing timelines out to ~10 years is a counterweight to the most aggressive AGI bets priced into AI-exposed equities and compute names; useful as a dampener on near-term 'AGI by 2027' narratives.
≈ “most of my timelines delay is just thinking that even getting to that point will take quite a while — quite a while, like a decade maybe, which I feel like in past years would have been called “extremely short timelines” and nowadays gets called “medium to long timelines.”” paraphrase
Shah expects an intelligence explosion to be a multi-year process (5-10 years from the crossover point), not months, because the first AI systems that can automate AI R&D will likely be more expensive than human researchers and only become cost-competitive gradually.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAGIAI R&D
So what: Implies the transformative-AI payoff curve is more gradual than 'fast takeoff' scenarios — supports a longer runway for incumbents and compute buildout rather than a sudden winner-take-all discontinuity.
≈ “the first automated systems that can really automate AI R&D will probably just be very expensive, to the point of potentially being more expensive than humans would be” paraphrase
Shah argues the popular 'superhuman coder triggers the intelligence explosion' thesis is overstated because a coding-specialized AI is just a better tool (like inventing the microscope) rather than a population of idea-generating researchers, and tool improvements historically produce straight-line, not hyperbolic, progress.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI R&Dsuperhuman coderMoore's law
So what: Reframes what milestone actually matters for a takeoff (AIs generating novel ideas across all of ML R&D, not just coding), giving investors a sharper signal to watch than coding-benchmark hype.
≈ “the superhuman coder is not something that can have ideas across the spectrum, across everything that happens in ML R&D. It has ideas within the realm of coding in particular” paraphrase
Shah frames the intelligence-explosion question through endogenous-growth economics (Kremer 1993): sustained progress depends on whether AI becomes a freely-copyable 'population' of researchers driving hyperbolic growth, versus merely a tool — and population growth (more idea-generators) is the crucial variable.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highKremer 1993endogenous growthGDP growth
So what: Gives a concrete macro lens for distinguishing real takeoff from incremental tooling gains — watch whether AI starts generating cross-domain novel ideas, which is the trigger for non-linear economic growth.
Shah is confident an intelligence explosion will eventually occur but only under heavy preconditions — most critically that AI becomes cheaper than humans for nearly all economically valuable tasks, which he stresses is 'not a given.'
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAGIlabor automation
So what: Cost-per-task parity (inference economics), not raw capability, is the gating variable for mass labor displacement — a cleaner metric for investors than capability benchmarks alone.
≈ “which, among other things implies that the AI is cheaper than the human, which I don’t think is a given” paraphrase
AI progress is continuous & measurable (no abrupt jumps) 4
Shah holds an 'approximate continuity assumption': AI capability progress is smooth and forecastable with respect to compute and labor inputs (even if calendar-time speed varies), so a model becoming 'unexpectedly capable' between releases is very unlikely.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindFrontier Safety Framework
So what: Underpins the view that capability roadmaps are predictable — reduces tail risk of a surprise capability shock that could violently re-rate AI markets, and supports the idea progress can be tracked quantitatively.
≈ “AI development progresses fairly smoothly and continuously” paraphrase
DeepMind worked with Epoch to build 'A Rosetta Stone for AI benchmarks,' stitching disparate benchmark scores into a single general-capability score from 2020 to today; plotted against release date it forms a near-straight line, indicating AI progress has been roughly linear, not accelerating.
data_point🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highEpoch AIGoogle DeepMindRosetta Stone for AI benchmarks
So what: A concrete, watchable instrument: if this composite line bends upward, that is the empirical early-warning of an intelligence explosion — a quantitative signal investors can monitor rather than relying on vibes.
≈ “when you start plotting this against release time, it’s just a nice line. It’s great. And you see that AI progress mostly looks pretty linear on this particular graph.” paraphrase
Shah dismisses the widespread 'AI progress is slowing down' perception, arguing the Epoch composite-benchmark line is still linear and that an actual slowdown or acceleration would be visually striking on that graph and not masked by benchmark gaming.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumEpoch AIAI benchmarks
So what: Counters the bearish 'scaling has plateaued' thesis circulating in markets; suggests steady underlying capability gains continue even if individual releases feel incremental.
Shah was not surprised by the o1/o3 reasoning-model wave because he had expected reinforcement learning to be applied to LLMs since ~2019; his timelines barely moved because RL did NOT generalize beautifully across all tasks the way instruction-following did.
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highOpenAIo1o3reinforcement learningreasoning models
So what: Generalization (not benchmark wins on RL-targeted tasks) is the real signal for an AGI re-rate; investors should discount narrow-benchmark breakthroughs that don't transfer.
≈ “it could have been the case that once we do reinforcement learning it just generalises beautifully to everything — the same way that instruction following really does generalise beautifully to everything — and actually that was not the case” paraphrase
Compute, inference scaling & the cost curve 3
Shah says investment has shifted heavily toward inference-time compute / test-time scaling over the past year (citing Google's Deep Think algorithm) and expects this trend to continue, with the first automated AI researchers being compute-hungry systems that explore many dead ends.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogleDeep Thinkinference computetest-time scaling
So what: Reinforces sustained demand for inference compute (chips, datacenters, energy) even past pretraining-scaling debates — supportive for the inference-serving and accelerator supply chain rather than just training clusters.
≈ “there’s just been way more investment in inference time compute, inference time scaling. Google has a Deep Think algorithm which applies even more inference scaling to get even better results.” paraphrase
Shah predicts the cost of automated AI research will fall quickly once achieved ('as is usually the case in AI'), so the acceleration begins when AI crosses human cost-parity and then becomes progressively cheaper — making cost-per-research-unit the key acceleration trigger.
causal🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI R&Dinference compute
So what: Falling inference cost is the mechanism that converts capability into economic disruption; the inflection to watch is AI research cost dropping below human researcher cost.
Pretraining remains by far the most powerful method for instilling capability, and its heavy reliance on parallel GPU/TPU computation structurally forces low 'opaque serial depth' — a hardware-economic constraint, not a temporary artifact.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGPUsTPUspretrainingtransformers
So what: Ties model interpretability/safety durability directly to GPU/TPU architecture economics; as long as parallel-compute hardware dominates, models stay structurally legible — relevant to both safety and chip-demand narratives.
≈ “GPUs and TPUs are very powerful computationally, but the way that they get this power is by doing a tonne of computations in parallel” paraphrase
DeepMind vs OpenAI/Anthropic positioning 4
Shah says he trusts Google/DeepMind's public safety statements more than Anthropic's or OpenAI's because Google is 'paranoid' about commitments and only states what it will actually do, citing that DeepMind's first Frontier Safety Framework deliberately avoided the word 'commit.'
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindAnthropicOpenAIFrontier Safety Framework
So what: Insider signal on relative corporate credibility/governance culture across the three frontier labs — relevant for assessing reputational/regulatory risk exposure in Alphabet vs private peers.
≈ “This is one place where I do actually trust Google more than Anthropic — or OpenAI, for that matter.” paraphrase
DeepMind is hiring 'quite a bit less' than Anthropic and OpenAI right now, with only a couple of safety-relevant roles open, contrasting with peers hiring 'hand over fist.'
data_point🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Hiring intensity is a proxy for capital deployment and expansion posture; DeepMind's restraint vs aggressive peer hiring signals differing burn/scaling strategies among the labs.
≈ “No, I think we are hiring quite a bit less than Anthropic and OpenAI at the moment.” paraphrase
Anthropic's models are the least overfit to public benchmarks, so the Epoch composite score tends to UNDER-estimate Claude relative to peers — i.e. Claude is better than its benchmark scores suggest while open-source models are likely more benchmark-overfit.
data_point🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumAnthropicClaudeopen-source modelsbenchmarks
So what: Benchmark leaderboards systematically misrank labs; investors comparing model quality by public benchmarks should discount benchmark-optimized players and credit Anthropic's real-world capability.
≈ “it does tend to underestimate Claude or Anthropic’s models relative to models from other providers — because generally Claude models, since they are not overfit to the benchmarks, will tend to underperform on benchmarks relative to how good the model actually is” paraphrase
DeepMind's safety/alignment research is based in London and tends to 'fly under the radar' versus the Bay Area where word-of-mouth dominates; Shah names underappreciated DeepMind papers (MONA on reward hacking, the AGI safety position paper, Rosetta Stone benchmarks).
anecdote🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumGoogle DeepMindLondonBay Area
So what: Geographic distance from the Bay Area information network means DeepMind's technical edge is systematically underweighted in industry discourse — a possible perception gap vs reality on Alphabet's AI capabilities.
Safety → regulation: auditing over commitments & eval mandates 6
Shah argues binding public safety 'commitments' from AI companies don't work because firms can and do walk them back (citing Anthropic dropping 'commitment' language across RSP versions), and rules written in advance are too rigid for fast-changing research.
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAnthropicResponsible Scaling PolicyAI regulation
So what: Insider skepticism that voluntary commitments constrain labs — suggests regulatory risk to AI firms is lower than commitment rhetoric implies, but also that accountability will have to come from elsewhere (auditors).
≈ “Intelligence and optimisation pressure applied against a rule will always get around the rule.” paraphrase
Shah favors third-party auditors/evaluators with deep technical access (modeled on Federal Reserve / Bank of England continuous bank supervision) as the right oversight mechanism, naming the AI Lab Watch scorecard as his favorite emerging accountability tool.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI Lab WatchZach Stein-PerlmanFederal ReserveBank of EnglandUS AISIUK AISI
So what: Points to the likely shape of future AI oversight — expert technical auditors and government safety institutes (AISIs) rather than blunt rules; the Fed-style model implies a heavily-resourced, embedded-supervisor regulatory regime is plausible.
≈ “I think both the Bank of England and the Federal Reserve in the US are really heavily involved with all of the banks: monitoring their books, basically in a constant conversation with them” paraphrase
Shah opposes mandatory pre-deployment evaluations as the dominant safety strategy, arguing they impose large costs on launch schedules while continuous progress plus a 'safety buffer' lets you rely on evals of the prior model — the approach DeepMind already uses in its Frontier Safety Framework.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindFrontier Safety Frameworkpre-deployment evals
So what: A DeepMind safety lead arguing against the policy approach much of the safety/regulatory community favors — signals labs will resist eval-gating that slows product launches, lowering the odds of launch-delaying regulation.
≈ “launch schedules are just really quite important at a company, and you try to keep them as short as possible” paraphrase
For misalignment/loss-of-control specifically, Shah says the real threat model is INTERNAL deployment (where a model gets permissions and access), not external product release, so pre-external-deployment evals miss the more dangerous scenario.
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindAI control
So what: Redirects where catastrophic risk actually lives — internal agentic deployments inside labs — which is where future controls/regulation will need to focus, not consumer-facing model cards.
Shah rejects safety asks that merely 'signal allegiance to the x-risk community' and warns that pressure to look good (e.g. logging hours spent on a model) creates perverse incentives toward measuring inputs over outcomes — he will simply refuse such requests.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI safety communityGoogle DeepMind
So what: Reveals genuine friction between frontier labs and the safety-advocacy community; tempers expectations that external pressure campaigns reliably change lab behavior.
≈ “there’s things I would categorise as pay costs to signal allegiance to the x-risk safety community. And I’m not about those. I don’t want to do them. If you ask for them, I’m just going to say no.” paraphrase
Shah pushes back on Zvi Mowshowitz's critique of Gemini 3's Frontier Safety Report, clarifying the cyber 'shortcut' was counted as a success (toward an 11/12 score) not waved away, and that the report's purpose is a formal safety determination, not a detailed reproducible audit document.
anecdote🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGemini 3Zvi MowshowitzFrontier Safety ReportCBRN
So what: Direct insider rebuttal to a prominent safety critic on Gemini 3 — clarifies that model cards are determinations, not transparency instruments, shaping how to read any lab's safety disclosures.
Misalignment risk: lower than headlines, but real 3
Despite being among the earliest AGI-safety researchers (since 2017), Shah believes catastrophic misalignment is plausible but unlikely-by-default, because no current argument compellingly shows it happens by default and RL is typically run over week-to-month horizons, not the long horizons needed for world-takeover goals.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAGIreinforcement learningmisalignment
So what: A credentialed insider giving a relatively optimistic base rate on catastrophic AI risk — a counterweight to doom narratives that could otherwise weigh on AI-sector sentiment and invite heavy-handed regulation.
≈ “we’re not going to do reinforcement learning over the course of one-year trajectories. Maybe we’re going to do reinforcement learning over a week or a month at most.” paraphrase
Shah says today's steerable, helpful models provide little reassurance about future safety because current systems haven't yet hit the regime that worried him (superhuman models reasoning in ways humans can't follow), so success on current alignment isn't strong evidence for future success.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI alignmentfrontier models
So what: Cautions against extrapolating current model docility into confidence about superhuman systems — the hard safety problems are still ahead, tempering 'alignment is basically solved' optimism.
Shah expects 'evilness'/propensity (unlike capability) CAN shift sharply between models because it's a contingent property of post-training, so DeepMind does run pre-deployment propensity evals (e.g. will the model help write suicide notes or incite violence) and will block launches if numbers are bad.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindpost-trainingmodel propensity
So what: Distinguishes predictable capability from volatile behavioral safety — the latter is where a model could fail a launch gate, a real (if rare) product-release risk for labs.
Chain-of-thought monitoring & technical safety bets 4
Shah expects chain-of-thought monitoring (reading models' reasoning) to remain useful for ~4-5 years (his median), longer than most peers, because transformers must externalize hard reasoning into legible English tokens due to low 'opaque serial depth' forced by parallel GPU/TPU pretraining.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highchain-of-thought monitoringtransformersGPUsTPUs
So what: If CoT monitoring stays viable for years, a major safety lever survives longer than feared — reducing one near-term catastrophic-risk vector and the regulatory urgency that would follow its loss.
≈ “my median is probably like four years, five years, I don’t know” paraphrase
DeepMind plans for AI control (treating internally-deployed AIs as 'untrusted insiders' with separate identities, per-permission justifications, and CoT monitoring) to be the medium-term basis for arguing Gemini is safe against loss-of-control, building on Redwood Research's control framework.
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindGeminiRedwood ResearchAI controlBuck ShlegerisRyan Greenblatt
So what: Signals the actual safety architecture Alphabet will lean on for AGI-era deployment — infrastructure/security engineering, identifying AI-control tooling and security hiring as a growth area.
≈ “control is, in the medium term, probably how we are going to argue that Gemini is safe, at least for misalignment and internal deployments” paraphrase
DeepMind's MONA technique (myopic optimization with non-myopic approval) can prevent multi-step reward hacking even when an overseer couldn't detect it, and Shah speculates it could actually IMPROVE capabilities/sample-efficiency at current levels by giving granular credit assignment — though at the cost of more human input.
data_point🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumGoogle DeepMindMONAreward hackingreinforcement learning
So what: A safety method that may double as a capability/efficiency gain is exactly the kind of 'free' alignment that labs will adopt — watch for safety techniques that don't trade off against performance, as those diffuse fastest.
Shah suggests an internal or industry-wide governance standard could cap a model's 'opaque serial depth' to preserve monitorability, and says DeepMind is publishing a paper (with JAX code) showing how to compute opaque serial depth for any architecture.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumGoogle DeepMindJAXopaque serial depthAI governance
So what: Hints at a concrete, measurable industry safety standard that could become a soft regulatory norm — a watchable governance development that could shape model-architecture choices across labs.
Governance gap & second-order societal effects 3
Shah's top concern is that AI will dramatically accelerate capabilities and technical alignment research but NOT governance, because governance requires different skills and 'governance people' (unlike capabilities researchers) are unlikely to want to use AI to accelerate their own work — leaving oversight lagging.
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI governanceForethought ResearchCoefficient GivingAjeya Cotra
So what: Flags a structural lag: technology races ahead while institutional oversight stalls — the macro risk vector most likely to produce disorderly, surprise regulation or instability that markets aren't pricing.
≈ “capabilities researchers want to actually accelerate themselves with AIs, and I don’t get the sense that governance people will want to do this” paraphrase
Shah argues the binding bottleneck to any global AI pause is that people don't yet agree AI will take over the world, so the highest-leverage move is generating decisive scientific evidence (which he expects will mostly show scaling is safe) rather than advocacy to slow down.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI pauseScott AlexanderAI safety
So what: Implies a near-term global pause/slowdown of AI development is unlikely absent dramatic new evidence — supportive of continued unconstrained capability/compute buildout in the medium term.
Shah takes seriously that the world could see 'a century's worth of progress in a decade,' implying a flood of unanticipated problems requiring flexible government intervention that today's rule-bound institutions are poorly equipped to handle.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumAI governancenational governments
So what: Compressed-progress scenario means rapid, hard-to-anticipate disruptions across sectors — a regime of elevated policy and macro uncertainty that favors adaptability over fixed long-horizon bets.
≈ “people talk about a century’s worth of progress in a decade” paraphrase
Labor, hiring & the AI-application deluge 2
Shah says the binding constraint at DeepMind's safety team is now implementation, not ideas — shifting hiring demand toward software engineers who 'do the obvious thing and land it' rather than researchers writing novel papers.
data_point🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindsoftware engineeringML research
So what: A leading-indicator on AI-lab labor demand: execution/SWE talent over pure research — signals where AI-sector hiring value is migrating as the field industrializes.
≈ “more of a focus on things like getting things done, software engineering, implementation now, relative to even just a year ago” paraphrase
DeepMind has been hit by a 'deluge of AI-assisted applications' that has degraded resume screening to the point that it is moving away from open hiring calls; even an LLM-captcha defense produces ~5% human false positives.
anecdote🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindLLMhiringrecruiting
So what: Concrete early evidence AI is breaking the hiring/credentialing market — a second-order effect that opens demand for AI-application-detection and verification tooling and may force application fees industry-wide.
≈ “we are a little bit less likely in the future to make these open calls for hiring, because it really is just such a pain to go and do the resume screening for all of them” paraphrase

This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis 1 episode(s) · 33 insights

This Startup Fused Human Brain Cells with Silicon Chips | E229566 min33 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Two deep-tech founder interviews: Cortical Labs (Hon Weng Chong) on commercializing 'biological computing' — silicon chips fused with lab-grown human neurons, now in a 120-unit 'biological data center' and raising $30M — and Pyka (Michael Norcia) on large autonomous electric/hybrid cargo and crop-spray drones deployed at scale in Brazil, with a defense-oriented hybrid-diesel 'Dropship' variant. Both surface the same investor thesis: hardware-software-fused systems gated by real-world deployment and regulation, not by code.
Cortical Labs commercial traction & business model · 5Biocomputing tech edge: sample efficiency & energy · 5Biocompute infrastructure, sovereignty & consciousness ethics · 5Developer ecosystem & accidental-platform thesis · 3Pyka: large autonomous drones & deployment moat · 5Regulation as the binding constraint on autonomous flight · 3Pyka Dropship: dual-use defense logistics · 3Vertical integration, supply chain & VC dynamics · 4
Cortical Labs commercial traction & business model 5
Cortical Labs sold out its entire capped run of 30 CL1 biological-computing units at ~$35,000 each (roughly $1M revenue), with units now deployed at five US institutions including Johns Hopkins, Mass General, UCSF, and Dartmouth.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO, 'Han')conviction: highCortical LabsJohns HopkinsMass GeneralUCSFDartmouthCL1
So what: Validated early demand and brand-name research customers de-risk a category most investors would dismiss as science fiction; the $35K hardware price anchors unit economics for the category.
“We've kind of exhausted our entire stock of 30 units that we had capped.” ▶ 1:43
Cortical Labs is raising a $30M round, framed around scaling its 'Cortical Cloud' rather than hardware sales, to lower the accessibility barrier to biological computing the way free CUDA did for GPUs.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical LabsCortical CloudNvidiaCUDA
So what: A live raise plus an explicit 'be the CUDA of wetware' platform thesis is the core investable narrative; watch whether cloud access compounds a developer ecosystem.
“we're raising 30” ▶ 33:38
90% of Cortical Labs' purchases come from academic R&D institutions, with stated primary use cases in drug discovery, disease modeling (Alzheimer's/dementia at Mass General), toxicology and animal-testing alternatives (Hopkins), and movement disorders (UCSF).
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical LabsMass GeneralJohns HopkinsUCSFdrug discoverytoxicology
So what: Near-term TAM is biomedical research / animal-testing replacement, not AI compute; the 'net good' framing also serves as regulatory/ethical cover for the more speculative compute use case.
Cortical Labs has deployed 120 CL1 units in its Melbourne 'biological data center' but only ~20 cloud users are live (with ~10 more pending), citing 'teething' issues growing neurons and a ~2-month lead time from cell-culture decision to usable compute.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsMelbourneCortical CloudCL1
So what: Biological supply-chain latency is the real bottleneck on revenue ramp; capacity utilization (~20/120) is the key operational metric to track before the next raise.
“while we do have 120, only about 20 or 30 users are on the cloud at the moment. Actually, no, 20 users.”
Each CL1 currently serves one customer/workload at a time (no chaining yet), but Cortical Labs is developing PDMS microfluidic chiplet partitioning to virtualize a single chip into many isolated neuron wells — a VMware-style multi-tenancy play to raise yield per unit.
thesis🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsPDMSmicrofluidicsVMwareCL1
So what: Multi-tenant virtualization would step-change cloud gross margins and is the path from boutique hardware to scalable SaaS-like economics; it is still R&D, not shipping.
“maybe you can get a hyper like a virtual machine with multiple people running the same thing. So that way we can increase yield”
Biocomputing tech edge: sample efficiency & energy 5
In unpublished reinforcement-learning path-finding benchmarks with a research partner, Cortical Labs' neurons were ~5,000x more sample-efficient than the partner's GPU-based RL systems, meaning a biological unit learns in one step what a GPU needs ~5,000 steps to learn.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical Labsreinforcement learningGPUNeurIPS
So what: Sample efficiency is the exact pain point in modern RL/robotics; a verified 5,000x edge (pending NeurIPS-style publication later this year) would be a genuine non-obvious threat to GPU-bound RL workloads.
“the neurons we had were 5,000 times more sample efficient than their GPU based systems.”
GPUs can offset their RL sample-inefficiency by accelerating simulated time ~5,000x, but that advantage collapses for embodied/robotic agents that must operate at real-world speed — pointing biological compute at robotics RL specifically.
causal🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumGPUreinforcement learningrobotics
So what: Identifies the wedge where biocompute could beat silicon: real-time embodied learning where GPUs can't fast-forward the clock; a watch-item for robotics-RL investors.
“you can't accelerate time if you're a robot. You you're operating at the same speed like everyone else in the physical world.”
A single CL1 unit draws only ~30 watts, letting data-center partners add biological compute without touching their power and cooling budgets — a structural advantage as AI data centers hit energy caps.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical LabsCL1data centerDay OneSingapore
So what: In a power-constrained AI buildout, ~30W compute that bolts onto existing racks under fixed megawatt caps is a real commercial hook for data-center operators.
“one unit only uses about 30 watts of energy” ▶ 12:50
Chong frames biology — even a fly or cockroach — as possessing 'generalized intelligence' (e.g. real-time evasion/prediction) that today's 'super-intelligent but not generally intelligent' LLMs like GPT-5.5 lack, positioning biocompute as a path to AGI-adjacent generalization without human-level neuron counts.
contrarian🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumLLMsGPT-5.5AGICortical Labs
So what: A contrarian framing that generalization, not raw scale, is the missing ingredient — if it holds, it reframes where AGI capex should flow away from pure transformer scaling.
“even very simple organisms like you know a fly has generalized intelligence um something that none of our machines have”
Chong argues the current limit on biological computing is algorithmic (how to encode digital/internet information into analog neural systems), not hardware — the same write-to-neuron problem that brain-computer-interface firms Neuralink and Synchron are solving.
framework🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsNeuralinkSynchronbrain-computer interface
So what: Casts Cortical Labs as adjacent to/benefiting from BCI R&D (Neuralink, Synchron); algorithmic breakthroughs there could be read-throughs for biocompute, and vice versa.
Biocompute infrastructure, sovereignty & consciousness ethics 5
Cortical Labs' next biological data center is in Singapore via data-center partner Day One, sited under a 200MW government energy cap, scaling to ~1,000 CL1 units (~200,000 neurons each), with an on-site lab to grow neurons locally.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical LabsDay OneSingaporeCL1
So what: A named data-center partner and a 10x capacity jump (120 to ~1,000 units) is the concrete scaling milestone; Day One's motivation (compute outside the power budget) is a reusable go-to-market for the category.
“they've built not just the space to house a thousand CR1 units, but next to it a laboratory for us to grow the cells for the compute on site.”
Growing neurons on-site decouples each biological data center from a single overseas chip vendor, making each facility self-sufficient and removing supply-chain constraints — a 'sovereign compute' angle distinct from GPU-vendor concentration.
thesis🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical Labssupply chaindata center
So what: Pitches biocompute as a vendor-independent, geopolitically resilient alternative to Nvidia-dependent AI infrastructure — a differentiated narrative for sovereign-AI and supply-chain-anxious buyers.
“every data center technically is not reliant on one vendor from one country.”
CL1 neurons can live a long time if maintained; the recurring consumable is the filtration ('kidney') tube-set cartridges, swapped every 4–6 months — defining the razor-and-blade aftermarket of the platform.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsCL1
So what: Consumable cartridge replacement is the recurring-revenue / maintenance-cost line item that determines true cloud margins and customer lock-in.
Cortical Labs draws a hard 'red line' against creating conscious systems because a conscious system could suffer, using stem-cell-derived neurons (not harvested brains) and engaging bioethicists and even the Vatican proactively to pre-empt ethical backlash.
thesis🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical LabsVaticanbioethicsstem cells
So what: Proactive ethics engagement is a deliberate regulatory/PR moat for a category one scandal could kill; the consciousness 'red line' is both genuine constraint and de-risking narrative for institutional buyers and regulators.
“You do not want to create conscious systems because um ethically uh a conscious system has the ability to suffer”
Chong says the industry's first priority is agreeing on shared nomenclature across the biocomputing and consciousness research fields, because without common definitions there's 'a lot of fear with not much understanding' that stalls progress and adoption.
framework🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical Labsbiocomputingconsciousness research
So what: Standards-setting is an early-mover advantage; whoever frames the nomenclature shapes regulation and category perception — a soft moat for the incumbent.
Developer ecosystem & accidental-platform thesis 3
A non-biologist student developer built a working 'Doom on neurons' demo at a Stanford hackathon using only Cortical Labs' API/SDK (pip-installable, Python/Jupyter), and was subsequently accepted into a top incubator — evidence the platform is low-barrier enough for outside developers.
anecdote🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsDoomStanfordPythonJupyter
So what: External-developer adoption with no biology background is the leading indicator of a real platform; the cloud + SDK is the flywheel the $30M raise is meant to fund.
“the uh Doom was actually a student developer um who um uh participated in a hackathon at Stanford. no biology background, used our APN SDK”
Chong argues today's AI boom was a 'fluke' — it took ~7 years from CUDA's creation to AlexNet — and Nvidia's real masterstroke was making CUDA free on even the cheapest gaming GPUs, an accessibility playbook Cortical Labs is explicitly copying with its cloud.
framework🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highNvidiaCUDAAlexNetCortical Labs
So what: Reframes Nvidia's moat as distribution/accessibility rather than serendipity, and signals Cortical Labs' deliberate strategy to seed an ecosystem before the 'killer algorithm' arrives.
“what Jensen did you know I think brilliantly at the start was he made CUDA free.”
Chong is explicitly 'team human,' arguing LLMs will not solve biocomputing applications and human developers remain essential to invent use cases — flagging upcoming hackathons at MIT to recruit talent.
thesis🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsLLMsMIT
So what: Talent/developer recruiting is the gating input for ecosystem growth; hackathon pipeline at MIT is a concrete signal of where the next applications (and hires) come from.
“I'm on team human here, so we still need developers to think of really cool applications. The LMS are not going to solve this for us.”
Pyka: large autonomous drones & deployment moat 5
Pyka claims to be the only company on Earth deploying Group 4 UAS (drones over 1,320 lbs) to commercial customers at scale, operating exclusively in rural Brazil where customers run 7-day-a-week, 12–13-hour shifts.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaGroup 4 UASBrazilagricultural drones
So what: A defensible 'only at scale' position in heavy autonomous cargo/ag drones; real-world operating hours are the proprietary data moat competitors lack.
“we're actually the only company on Earth that I'm aware of that have deployed a group for UAS to commercial customers at scale.”
Pyka's Pelican ag drone starts at ~$550,000 with a roughly 2–3 year payback, but its core value is spray quality and a cheaper labor pool — operators trainable in 2–4 weeks versus ~18 months for a manned aerial-application pilot.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaPelicanagricultural drones
So what: Concrete unit economics and a labor-arbitrage moat (weeks vs. 18 months of pilot training) define the adoption case; payback period is the metric for ag-tech ROI underwriting.
“you can train someone to operate a Pelican in 2 to 4 weeks versus a aerial application pilot is you know 18 months”
The chemical sprayed costs ~4x the cost of application, so Pyka's edge is even, precise deposition that reduces waste of an expensive input — amplified now that a blocked Strait of Hormuz is spiking global fertilizer prices.
causal🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaPelicanfertilizerStrait of Hormuz
So what: Ties drone ROI to volatile agrochemical input costs; rising fertilizer/chemical prices (Hormuz disruption) strengthen the precision-spray value prop and demand timing.
“the cost of the chemical that is being sprayed is typically about four times the cost of the application.”
Pyka's electric Pelican burns ~2 gallons/hour even when charged off a dirty diesel generator, versus the competing manned Air Tractor's ~55 gallons of jet fuel per hour — a ~25x fuel-efficiency advantage.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaPelicanAir Tractor
So what: Order-of-magnitude operating-cost advantage versus the incumbent crop-duster aircraft is the hard quantitative case for switching, fuel-price-resilient.
“our uh the Pelican in the worst case if you're charging it off of a diesel generator is about 2 gall per hour.”
US adoption requires ~20,000-acre farms to fully utilize a Pelican (very large by US standards, midsize by Brazil's), pointing to a 'spray-as-a-service' contractor model in the US where one operator services many smaller farms in a radius.
thesis🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: mediumPykaPelicanspray-as-a-serviceUnited States
So what: Signals the US business model will be service/utility, not unit sales — relevant for sizing US TAM and the eventual recurring-revenue structure.
Regulation as the binding constraint on autonomous flight 3
Pyka holds the largest drone approved for US commercial use by the FAA, but is capped at ~4km from takeoff (vs. 5–15km in Brazil), which Norcia calls 'not commercially viable today'; beyond-visual-line-of-sight rules are the gating constraint and Pyka is working with the FAA to expand the limit.
causal🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaFAABVLOSBrazil
So what: US regulatory timeline — not technology — is the swing factor for Pyka's domestic revenue; FAA BVLOS rulemaking is the catalyst to watch for the whole large-drone sector.
“We can only fly the drone about 4 kilometers away from where it took off and landed from, which is not commercially viable today.”
Brazil deregulated agricultural drones ~2 years ago, which is the primary reason Pyka deploys there; Norcia argues the US is 'shooting itself in the foot' by failing to enable scaled BVLOS operations on a relevant timeline.
causal🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaBrazilFAABVLOS
So what: Regulatory arbitrage (Brazil, Rwanda for Zipline, Ukraine for combat drones) is where real-world deployment data is being captured — a recurring pattern investors can use to find deployable hardware companies.
“Brazil actually deregulated agricultural drones about 2 years ago, which is very convenient for us.”
Norcia generalizes that hardware-software-blended products' value is '100% determined by their exposure to the real world,' citing SpaceX (blew up rockets to learn), Zipline (Rwanda), and Ukraine combat-drone makers as the few firms that found a path to collect deployment data.
framework🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highSpaceXZiplinePykaUkraine
So what: A reusable diligence lens: deep-tech hardware is only as valuable as its real-world flight/operating data — screen for companies that have unlocked a deployment geography.
“their value is 100% determined by their exposure to the real world.” ▶ 58:55
Pyka Dropship: dual-use defense logistics 3
Pyka built its hybrid turbo-diesel 'Dropship' from CAD to first flight in 180 days as a dual-use logistics aircraft, hitting Air Force/SOCOM/Army-derived requirements: ~1,000-mile range with 500-lb payload, fits a 20-ft container, removable tail, heavy-fuel (JP5/diesel) operation, and air-droppable payloads.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaDropshipUS Air ForceSOCOMUS Army
So what: A defense-spec product hitting unwritten contested-logistics requirements opens a far larger DoD TAM than ag; 180-day CAD-to-flight is the speed signal that attracts defense-tech capital.
“we went from uh like initial CAD renderings to first flight in 180 days.”
Pyka sent three electric Pelican Cargo aircraft to the Air Force and one to the Army, received positive feedback, a follow-on contract interest from the Air Force, and invitations to major demos in ~2.5 months — concrete early defense traction.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaPelican CargoUS Air ForceUS Army
So what: Tangible government pilot deployments and follow-on contract signals are leading indicators of a defense revenue ramp; the upcoming demos are a near-term catalyst.
Pyka deliberately did not optimize Dropship as a low-cost kinetic bomber — its vehicles are 'too nice,' reliable and long-lived — choosing the reusable contested-logistics niche over expendable munitions, a different defense positioning than attritable attack drones.
thesis🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: mediumPykaDropshipdefense
So what: Clarifies Pyka's defense segment as reusable logistics, not the crowded expendable-FPV-strike market — distinct competitive set and procurement logic.
“our vehicles last too long. Basically, they're too nice.” ▶ 52:53
Vertical integration, supply chain & VC dynamics 4
Pyka vertically integrated nearly every critical component (motors, batteries, motor controllers, avionics, airframes) because off-the-shelf parts didn't exist when it started, letting it now ship NDAA-compliant versions and manufacture battery management systems in the US for defense.
thesis🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaNDAAsupply chainChina
So what: China-free, NDAA-compliant vertical integration is a defense procurement moat and de-risks supply chains — a key diligence checkbox for defense-hardware investors.
“we actually vertically integrated essentially every critical component on our product.”
Norcia quantifies the cost of supply-chain sovereignty: if you own the design, US vs. China manufacturing is ~2x; if you don't own the design and buy from an OEM, US vs. China is ~4x — so design ownership roughly halves the reshoring cost penalty.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaChinaUnited StatesOEM
So what: A concrete reshoring cost framework: owning IP cuts the US-manufacturing premium from ~4x to ~2x — useful for evaluating any hardware firm's China-decoupling economics.
“If you don't own the design and you're buying from an OEM... it's probably gonna be like 4x difference.” ▶ 1:01:30
Norcia says current VC capital is flowing heavily to 'enterprise agent orchestration / MCP server' and AI-wrapper SaaS startups (which he urges VCs to stop backing), implying capital is mispriced away from deployable deep-tech hardware like drones.
contrarian🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: mediumventure capitalMCPAI SaaSPyka
So what: A founder-side read on capital rotation: crowded AI-agent/SaaS funding vs. relatively under-capitalized deep-tech hardware — a potential contrarian allocation signal.
“Stop backing SAS companies that are slapping on an AI rapper. Back something cool. We're building drones, y'all.”
Norcia argues the most successful hardware companies (Apple, Tesla, DJI) win by owning both hardware and software to deliver a 'magical' simple experience, and warns that integrating disparate third-party systems produces a visibly worse product — implicitly framing Boeing's outsource-and-integrate model as the cautionary failure.
framework🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highAppleTeslaDJIBoeingPyka
So what: A reusable thesis that full-stack hardware-software ownership is the durable moat in hardware (Apple/Tesla/DJI) and outsourced integration is the path to Boeing-style decline — a screen for hardware investments.
“you have to own the hardware and the software in my opinion. like that's how you make that really magical experience.”

Decouple · Chris Keefer 1 episode(s) · 30 insights

Limits to Growth of LLMs w/ David Helmer92 min30 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
AI consultant David Helmer argues the LLM boom is a bubble: scaling laws are hitting diminishing returns, hallucinations/reliability are architecturally unfixable for high-consequence use cases (only code is a partial exception), and trillion-dollar IPO valuations for OpenAI/Anthropic/SpaceX rest on an AGI story with no profitability behind it. The downstream risk is severe market concentration in the Mag 7 plus circular vendor financing — but he thinks data-center-driven nuclear/power demand may persist on sovereign-power and energy-security grounds even if the AI bubble bursts.
Scaling-law breakdown & where capability gains now come from · 4Reliability & hallucinations as an architectural ceiling · 6Compute economics & the unit-cost / monetization problem · 5Bubble, trillion-dollar IPOs & market-concentration risk · 7Datacenter power demand & the nuclear thesis · 4Misalignment, agent risk & where the real harms are · 4
Scaling-law breakdown & where capability gains now come from 4
The LLM 'scaling law' is not a true law and is hitting diminishing returns — throwing more data and compute at transformer models is no longer the main source of capability gains.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLMstransformersscaling lawscompute
So what: Undercuts the core investment thesis that more GPUs/datacenters reliably buy better models; watch capex-justification narratives at OpenAI/Nvidia for cracks.
“It's not clear that that scaling law is going to continue to hold. It's not a law in the way of other things that scale where you know every time it gets bigger you get this much better.”
The most interesting recent AI advances come from neuro-symbolic methods — adding deterministic rules and symbolic reasoning to LLMs — not from raw scale, which Helmer expects to define future direction.
prediction🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highneuro-symbolic AIsymbolic reasoningLLMs
So what: Capital concentrated purely in scale (mega-clusters, frontier-model training) may be misallocated vs. cheaper hybrid approaches; favors efficiency plays over brute-force compute.
≈ “most of the really interesting recent advances haven't come from scale... putting more symbolic reasoning and and hard deterministic rules into this and marrying them” paraphrase ▶ 27:43
Successive frontier releases (e.g. GPT-5 vs GPT-4) are no longer dramatically better than predecessors, signaling a capability plateau rather than a linear rise toward AGI.
data_point🗣 host (Chris Keefer) / guestconviction: mediumGPT-5GPT-4OpenAIAGI
So what: A visible plateau removes the 'next model changes everything' option value that underpins valuations.
'AGI' and 'super intelligence' have no agreed definition and function mainly as marketing terms; AI is already superhuman at many narrow tasks without needing a city's worth of power.
contrarian🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAGIsuper intelligence
So what: Frames AGI hype as a fundraising device, not a roadmap — a tell for spotting promotional vs. substantive AI claims.
≈ “There is no such thing first as as AGI or super intelligence... first ask somebody to define it because you'll find that it doesn't really mean anything” paraphrase
Reliability & hallucinations as an architectural ceiling 6
Hallucinations and reliability failures are embedded in ML/LLM architecture and cannot be closed where outputs can't be deterministically verified — every transcription/summarizer tool's terms of use put error liability on the user precisely because vendors have no path to eliminate it.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLMshallucinationsmachine learning
So what: Caps the addressable enterprise market to use cases tolerant of error; high-consequence verticals (medical, legal, finance) carry liability that erodes the value prop.
“why do the terms of use always say that errors aren't their fault?”
Agent reliability compounds catastrophically: a per-task error rate applied across a 40-step workflow makes end-to-end success collapse, so agentic deployments fail exactly where they'd replace workers.
framework🗣 host (Chris Keefer)conviction: highAI agentsagentic workflows
So what: Multi-step agent automation — the basis for labor-replacement ROI claims — is mathematically fragile; discount revenue projections built on agent autonomy.
≈ “if you have a agent that's got a 95% reliability... they have to do a workflow of 40 tasks, if you kind of multiply that out, that becomes a real problem” paraphrase
Updating to a newer model does not monotonically reduce hallucinations — sometimes hallucination rates go up — so 'the next version fixes it' is unreliable.
causal🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLMsmodel updateshallucinations
So what: Removes the assumption that error rates trend monotonically to zero, weakening forward reliability projections.
“sometimes when they update the model, hallucination rates, as you think of them, go up”
Guardrails are inference-based and therefore inherently breakable — jailbreaks via iambic-pentameter poetry, 'it's just a dream' framing, or Morse-code prompts repeatedly defeat safety, making it a permanent whack-a-mole rather than a solvable problem.
causal🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLM guardrailsprompt injectionAI safety
So what: Unfixable guardrails create open-ended legal/liability tail risk for any agent touching money or regulated decisions.
“anytime you have an inference-based thing... you can't guarantee the safeguard. That's why the suicide things fail.” ▶ 43:23
Prompt-injection of agents has already caused real losses — one agent was tricked into giving away hundreds of thousands of dollars via hidden Morse code, and at least three production databases have been deleted by rogue agents.
data_point🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAI agentsprompt injectioncybersecurity
So what: Because agents operate at scale, a single vulnerability propagates fast — a structural risk for fintech/enterprise agent deployments and their insurers.
≈ “one person tricked an agent into giving away hundreds of thousands of dollars by putting Morse code in the prompt” paraphrase
Code is the one near-exception to the reliability problem because outputs can be deterministically tested (CI/CD, vulnerability scans, test suites) and wrapped in neuro-symbolic guardrails — but even there, large production codebases, maintenance, and rerun costs make the value prop ambiguous.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highClaude Codecoding copilotsCI/CDproduction code
So what: If code is the only robust frontier-model use case, the TAM justifying trillion-dollar valuations is far narrower than priced.
“code being sort of a a weird case... the positive use cases are much harder to find than the negative ones” ▶ 53:16
Compute economics & the unit-cost / monetization problem 5
Per-user economics are negative: every added user currently costs the labs money, and $20/month consumer subscriptions don't cover capex on GPUs and datacenters — Helmer frames a roughly 10-order-of-magnitude gap between revenue and full infrastructure cost.
data_point🗣 host (Chris Keefer) / guestconviction: highOpenAIAnthropichyperscalersGPUsdatacenters
So what: Negative marginal economics with no obvious price escalation path is the bear core; pricing power is the key variable to watch.
“every user that they add is currently costing them money” ▶ 1:18:09
There is little willingness to pay frontier-model prices for low-value tasks — nobody pays hundreds of dollars to write emails faster — so raising subscription prices to reach profitability risks demand collapse.
causal🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLM pricingenterprise SaaS
So what: The monetization ceiling, not the tech ceiling, is the binding constraint; pricing experiments (e.g. Anthropic token-count changes that 'almost' caused a revolt) are the canary.
“would you be willing to pay $250 an email to write better” ▶ 1:21:15
GPUs in datacenters have brutal economics — high first-year infant-mortality failure, and effectively none lasting to three years — so datacenters are a continuous capex sink, not a one-time fiber-optic-style buildout.
data_point🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highGPUsdatacentersNvidia
So what: Recurring chip-replacement capex makes the railroad/fiber 'durable surplus infrastructure eventually gets used' analogy break down for AI compute.
“no you wish you got 5 years out of a chip in a data center... None of them are lasting to three years.” ▶ 1:17:31
OpenAI's Sora video-generation product was effectively pulled back because at scale it 'stopped being a loss leader and became a loss' — a tell that generative video economics don't work.
anecdote🗣 host (Chris Keefer) / guestconviction: mediumSoraOpenAIgenerative video
So what: Concrete evidence that even flagship generative features get cut on cost — a template for how unprofitable AI features unwind.
“your cat video stopped being a loss leader. It just became a loss”
Beyond infrastructure and compute, AI labs face large unbooked future costs: paying for training data, labor (e.g. Kenyan content moderators), and mounting legal liability — Anthropic's >$1B settlement for pirated books was reportedly rejected by the judge as too small.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAnthropicMetadata licensingcopyright litigation
So what: Today's cost base understates true unit economics; copyright settlements, injunctions, and data-payment obligations are a latent cost wave on top of compute.
≈ “Anthropic had a settlement for pirated books that to my understanding got kicked back by the judge because it wasn't enough at over a billion dollars” paraphrase
Bubble, trillion-dollar IPOs & market-concentration risk 7
Helmer's base case is that AI is a bubble: '95% of LLM investments failed to return value' (MIT study), and the fundamentals can't justify the valuations being floated.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highMIT studyLLMsAI bubble
So what: An explicit bubble call from a technical diligence advisor; the 95% failure stat is a citable bear data point.
≈ “MIT did a study last year where they said 95% of LLM investments failed to return value” paraphrase
OpenAI, Anthropic, and SpaceX are being lined up for ~trillion-dollar IPOs and possible accelerated S&P 500 inclusion without demonstrated profitability — Helmer likens these names to meme stocks trading at 10x–100x any defensible worth.
contrarian🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicSpaceXS&P 500IPO
So what: Accelerated index inclusion of unprofitable mega-caps would force passive flows into stretched valuations — a structural risk for index investors.
≈ “they're much closer to a meme stock than than they are to that at at this time” paraphrase
Anthropic's reported profit is suspect: it's non-GAAP, excludes GPU capital cost, and appears to lean on a one-quarter discounted-compute deal with SpaceX (~$1B+/month of compute) that reverts to full price afterward.
data_point🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAnthropicSpaceXGAAP accounting
So what: Headline 'profitability' may be an accounting artifact tied to a temporary discount — scrutinize whether the next quarter reverses it.
“they don't really tell you how they calculated it... they're not using GAP methods” ▶ 1:16:04
Circular vendor financing props up the boom — labs receive investment from Oracle/Amazon/Nvidia and spend it buying those same vendors' chips and cloud, echoing the dotcom bust; much of Nvidia's operating cash flow flows to customers who then buy Nvidia chips.
causal🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highNvidiaOracleAmazonAWSOpenAIdotcom bubble
So what: Circular financing inflates apparent demand; the vendor-customer feedback loop is the classic bubble fragility to monitor.
“you're paying Amazon back with their own money” ▶ 1:19:11
Market concentration is unprecedented: the Magnificent 7 plus prospective trillion-dollar AI IPOs dominate the index and account for much of recent GDP growth via the datacenter buildout, so a re-rating of the AI story would be 'brutal' for the broad market.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highMagnificent 7GoogleAmazonMicrosoftTeslaS&P 500
So what: Single-narrative concentration means an AI-thesis repricing is a whole-index event, not an isolated sector drawdown.
≈ “the degree of market concentration, I don't think we've ever really had that with this kind of exposure” paraphrase
A burst would not bankrupt all players — Google (search), Amazon (AWS), and Microsoft have real non-AI bases — but post-bust the glut of cloud compute everyone built would intensify competition and compress hyperscaler margins.
prediction🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: mediumGoogleAmazonAWSMicrosofthyperscalers
So what: Even survivors face margin compression from compute oversupply — a second-order short on hyperscaler cloud margins distinct from the equity drawdown.
“everybody has now invested in all of this compute... So, they suddenly have more competition than they used to. It's going to put pressure on their margins” ▶ 1:26:21
AI compute is unlike the dotcom fiber-optic overbuild because fiber was durable and got cheaply reused (enabling Amazon-style low-marginal-cost scaling), whereas GPUs depreciate in under three years and each AI user adds heavy marginal cost.
framework🗣 host (Chris Keefer) / guestconviction: highdotcom bubblefiber opticsGPUsAmazon
So what: The 'surplus infrastructure eventually pays off' bull comfort doesn't transfer — depreciating GPUs offer no durable residual value.
Datacenter power demand & the nuclear thesis 4
Keefer believes the sector is 'very solidly in a nuclear bubble,' evidenced by soaring, bumpy valuations of advanced-reactor names like Oklo, with overlap from AI-hype figures (Sam Altman took Oklo public via SPAC).
thesis🗣 host (Chris Keefer)conviction: highOkloSam AltmanSPACadvanced reactorsnuclear
So what: Nuclear equities are riding the same AI-demand narrative; if AI power demand evaporates, speculative reactor SPACs are most exposed.
≈ “I think we're very solidly in a nuclear bubble um as evidenced by some of the soaring valuations” paraphrase
AI-driven baseload power demand has catalyzed concrete nuclear restarts — Three Mile Island Unit 1, Palisades, and Duane Arnold — replacing climate as the dominant narrative driver under the Trump administration.
data_point🗣 host (Chris Keefer)conviction: highThree Mile IslandPalisadesDuane Arnoldnuclear restartshyperscalers
So what: These restarts are leveraged to AI demand assumptions; a compute-demand reset would directly threaten their economics and PPAs.
≈ “the restart of 3M island unit one palisades Dwayne Arnold a huge surge in interest in nuclear as a result of of the AI demand” paraphrase
If the AI bubble bursts and projected datacenter power demand evaporates, it undermines the case for new nuclear — but Helmer argues nuclear/clean-power demand may persist anyway on sovereign-power, energy-security, and re-industrialization grounds because governments pivot slowly.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: mediumnuclearenergy securitysovereign powerChinarenewables
So what: Decouples the longer-term nuclear/power thesis from the AI bubble — the durable bull case for power is geopolitical, not compute-driven.
“I don't know that an AI collapse necessarily means a collapse on that part of the energy market”
'Datacenters in space' is not happening — half the chips would die within a year with no maintenance possible — making it an unrealistic basis for valuations (e.g. SpaceX) that price in space datacenters and lunar colonization.
contrarian🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highSpaceXspace datacentersGPUs
So what: Flags speculative 'optionality' (space compute) baked into SpaceX's trillion-dollar valuation as non-credible.
“in a year half your chips are dead and you can't do maintenance in space” ▶ 1:17:43
Misalignment, agent risk & where the real harms are 4
The genuine near-term danger is not sentient super-AI but misaligned automated systems given real-world permissions at scale — social-media ad-revenue optimization is the canonical example of an objective-function gone wrong, not a sci-fi paperclip maximizer.
framework🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAI alignmentsocial mediaAI agentsNick Bostrom
So what: Reframes 'AI risk' as a permissions/governance failure mode — relevant for assessing operational and regulatory risk in agent deployments.
“this isn't theoretical. This is what social media is.”
AI doom and 'too dangerous to release' messaging functions to pump valuations and dodge liability — an 'effective altruism' framing of infinite future benefit lets companies discount present-day harms (e.g. the suicide cases) to near zero.
contrarian🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: higheffective altruismOpenAISam AltmanAI safetyEd Zitron
So what: Treats existential-risk PR as a marketing/liability instrument — a lens for discounting 'capabilities so dangerous' announcements.
≈ “The idea that AGI is coming and we must be there first is really helpful if you're trying to justify hundreds of billions of dollars of investment.” paraphrase
Anthropic's framing of Claude as a sentient 'entity' would be commercially convenient — it could shift legal accountability onto the AI — yet their own constitution lets the model register objections while still being forced to comply, undercutting the safety-leader positioning.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAnthropicClaudeAI sentienceliability
So what: Anthropic's safety/ethics branding is, in Helmer's read, inconsistent with its conduct (DoD deployment, waived safety commitments, shipped known-defective models) — relevant to reputational/regulatory risk pricing.
“if some of the companies actually believed what they were saying about these models being... entities, you would have to have pretty significant questions about what they're doing”
LLMs are excellent for fraud and low-trust scale attacks — a criminal sending a thousand scam emails needs only one to land — so the clearest reliable use cases skew negative (hacking, fraud) rather than positive enterprise value.
causal🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLMsfraudcybersecurity
So what: If the most error-tolerant, scalable use cases are malicious, the legitimate monetizable surface shrinks further — and cyber-defense becomes a growth adjacency.
≈ “if you are a criminal organization trying to defraud people, LBS are actually great” paraphrase

What Bitcoin Did · Peter McCormack 1 episode(s) · 28 insights

The State Has Become a Monster | Julian Jessop66 min28 insights2026-06-06
▶ Watch on YouTube
Classical-liberal economist Julian Jessop argues the UK's post-2008 stagnation is self-inflicted policy failure from an oversized state (40% of GDP, near-record tax burden, 90+ taxes), and lays out an investor-relevant map of UK fiscal/political risk: Reeves's tax-led fiscal headroom, bond-market sensitivity to a Starmer/Reeves exit, supply-side energy and planning failures, and a base case that the UK can outperform Europe on AI and light-touch regulation IF it avoids an EU regulatory 'reset.'
UK oversized-state thesis & supply-side stagnation · 5Labour-market & minimum-wage policy risk · 4Price controls & the war on markets · 3UK politics, gilts & market sensitivity · 5NHS, public-sector productivity & state IT failure · 4Comparative models: Milei, China, Singapore, Poland · 3UK base case & AI/Brexit optionality · 4
UK oversized-state thesis & supply-side stagnation 5
Julian Jessop argues UK productivity has been structurally weak since the 2008 global financial crisis and that this — not inequality or the fading IT revolution — is the core driver of stagnant living standards, framing the slowdown as a fixable policy failure rather than an inevitable structural decline.
thesis🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highUK economyproductivity2008 financial crisis
So what: Bull case for UK assets is contingent on a supply-side policy reversal; without it, expect continued low-growth, low-productivity drift that caps UK equity and currency upside.
“since then, pretty much everything has has gone horribly wrong” ▶ 1:16
Jessop says the UK state has grown to roughly 40% of national income and argues the appropriate size is closer to 20% — implying the entire current fiscal and regulatory footprint is roughly double what he considers efficient.
framework🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highUK government spendingGDPpublic goods
So what: Frames the magnitude of any credible 'small-state' reform program; investors should treat a halving of state share as the radical-bull scenario, not the base case.
“I state it's about 40% of of of national income taxing or or spending. Um, I think it could be maybe half that, maybe 20%”
Jessop identifies UK energy and housing supply as the root macro problem — the UK imports oil and gas (including North Sea gas drilled by Norway on the opposite side) and under-builds housing and infrastructure due to restrictive planning, rather than addressing demand-side symptoms.
causal🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highNorth Sea oil and gasNorwayUK housingplanning reformenergy market
So what: Domestic energy production and planning liberalisation are the key catalysts to watch for UK homebuilders, energy producers and infrastructure plays; continued under-supply keeps cost-of-living and inflation pressure structurally elevated.
“we're importing um oil and gas including you know oil and gas that Norway has dug out from the opposite side of the North Sea from us which makes no sense at all”
Jessop contends the UK has at least 90 separate taxes — including at least one levied on something that no longer exists — creating a large 'dead weight' that disproportionately burdens small businesses while entrenching large incumbents who can afford compliance teams and lobbyists.
data_point🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highUK tax systemsmall businesseslarge corporatescompliance
So what: Regulatory and tax complexity is a structural moat for large-cap UK incumbents versus SMEs; favours scaled players and is a headwind for UK small-cap/AIM exposure.
“we have at least you 90 separate taxes now in the UK” ▶ 21:48
Jessop frames a 'ratchet' mechanism in UK fiscal policy whereby spending commitments like the winter fuel payment and the state-pension triple lock are nearly impossible to remove once introduced, locking in structurally rising entitlement spending.
framework🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highwinter fuel paymentstate pension triple lockGordon Brownentitlement spending
So what: Entitlement ratchet implies the UK fiscal trajectory bends toward higher spending and taxes regardless of governing party — a structural negative for gilts and the long-run tax burden.
“anytime something is introduced it's then almost impossible to to take it away”
Labour-market & minimum-wage policy risk 4
Jessop attributes the recent spike in UK youth unemployment — rising at a pace last seen during COVID and the 2008 crisis — to government interventions, specifically the national minimum wage and day-one employment rights that make employers reluctant to hire inexperienced young workers.
causal🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highUK youth unemploymentnational minimum wageemployment rightslabour market
So what: Rising youth unemployment is a leading indicator of UK labour-market weakness; watch for further hiring deterioration in low-margin, labour-intensive sectors (hospitality, retail).
“unemployment amongst young people is has spiked again. Uh the last time it rose this quickly was was during COVID and before then it was during the global financial crisis”
Jessop describes the proposed £15/hour minimum wage and France's relatively generous minimum wage (with even higher youth unemployment than the UK) as cases where wage floors raise unemployment and hurt the intended beneficiaries.
contrarian🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highminimum wageFranceyouth unemployment
So what: A £15 minimum wage would pressure margins in UK hospitality/retail toward unviability per McCormack's own coffee-shop example; a watch-item for UK consumer-facing labour-intensive businesses.
“every historical example has shown that if you if you cap rents or massively increase minimum wages, it ends up hurting the very people you're supposed to help”
Peter McCormack states that a £15 minimum wage would push his coffee-shop business from viable to unviable, forcing layoffs — a first-hand operator data point on how wage-floor increases convert thin-margin UK hospitality businesses into loss-makers.
anecdote🗣 Peter McCormack (host, business owner)conviction: highminimum wageUK hospitalitysmall business
So what: Ground-truth on UK SME hospitality fragility under rising labour costs; reinforces the structural margin squeeze thesis for the sector.
“it would take the business from viable to unviable” ▶ 7:48
Jessop says Labour's manifesto pledge not to raise income tax, VAT or corporation tax forced it to raise employer National Insurance contributions instead — a 'tax on jobs' (Reeves's own prior phrase) that is now contributing to labour-market weakness.
causal🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highemployer National InsuranceRachel Reevesincome taxVATcorporation taxLabour
So what: Employer NIC increases are a direct margin and hiring headwind for UK labour-intensive employers; the manifesto tax-lock means further revenue will likely come through stealth/marginal taxes, raising policy unpredictability.
“As Rachel Ree herself said when she was shadow chancellor, it's a tax on jobs”
Price controls & the war on markets 3
Jessop warns of an escalating UK 'war on prices' — rent caps returning to favour, minimum-wage hikes, voluntary supermarket price caps, and courts deciding 'fair' comparative pay between shop and warehouse workers — arguing administered prices distort the signalling function markets rely on.
thesis🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highrent controlsprice capssupermarketsminimum wageequal pay litigation
So what: Spreading price administration raises regulatory risk for UK landlords, supermarkets and retail employers; rent caps specifically would reduce housing supply and quality, a negative for residential rental and homebuilder economics.
“there seems to be this war on prices” ▶ 27:52
Jessop argues Reform UK's proposal to exempt overtime above 40 hours/week from income tax — modelled on a US idea — would fail as it did in France, where well-off people with accountants reclassified hours as overtime, lower-paid workers gained nothing, and tax revenue fell.
causal🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highReform UKovertime tax exemptionFrancetax avoidance
So what: Signals that headline 'pro-grafter' tax giveaways are gameable and revenue-negative; investors should discount populist tax-cut pledges from both Reform and Greens as fiscally unfunded.
“all that happened is that relatively well-off people with clever accountants fiddled their arrangements to make sure that more of their hours counted as overtime”
Jessop frames regulatory complexity with the maxim that 'the bigger the surface area of the state, the more chance there is to exploit it,' arguing dense rules entrench large businesses (who can staff HR, legal and compliance) at the expense of small competitors.
framework🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highregulationlarge corporatesSMEscompliance
So what: Reinforces that rising UK regulation is a competitive moat for incumbents — structurally favours large-cap defensives over UK small-caps and new entrants.
“the bigger the surface area of the state, the more chance there is to exploit it”
UK politics, gilts & market sensitivity 5
Jessop says UK financial markets have reacted negatively every time Keir Starmer or Rachel Reeves looked at risk of leaving, because the market views the alternatives (e.g. Andy Burnham) as worse for fiscal stability — making Reeves's perceived commitment to fiscal rules a stabiliser for gilts and sterling.
data_point🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highKeir StarmerRachel ReevesAndy BurnhamgiltsUK bond market
So what: A leadership change away from Reeves is a tail risk for gilts and sterling; the bond market is effectively pricing Reeves as the fiscal-credibility anchor — watch Labour leadership signals as a gilt catalyst.
“every time there have been concerns that either Starmmer or Rachel Reeves might might go, the markets have reacted negatively”
Jessop notes Reeves built extra fiscal headroom into the budget but did so by raising taxes rather than controlling spending, which markets disliked even as they credit her commitment to the fiscal rules.
data_point🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumRachel Reevesfiscal headroomUK budgetfiscal rules
So what: Tax-led rather than spending-led consolidation keeps the UK tax burden rising and growth suppressed; the quality of any future headroom-building (taxes vs cuts) is a gilt and growth signal.
“she's done it by raising taxes, not by controlling spending”
Jessop argues the 2022 Truss/Kwarteng 'mini budget' bond-market blowup was already calming and was driven partly by unlucky timing — the Bank of England starting to sell gilts (QT) into a fragile market amid globally rising rates — rather than purely by the policy itself.
causal🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumLiz TrussKwasi KwartengBank of Englandquantitative tighteninggiltsmortgage market
So what: Reframes UK gilt fragility as partly a QT/liquidity timing problem; relevant for assessing how future unfunded fiscal moves could interact with BoE balance-sheet operations to trigger disorderly gilt selloffs.
“the bank of England was starting to sell government bonds back onto the market so the bond market was fragile interest rates were rising around the rest of the world”
Jessop says the Greens and Reform UK are between them polling over 50% by promising popular but unfunded policies, with Reform paradoxically favouring nationalisation and keeping the pension triple lock — i.e. expanding rather than shrinking the state.
data_point🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highGreen PartyReform UKnationalisationtriple lock
